proposals.title: Prophecy: Predictive Prefetching and Tiered Orchestration for Latency-Hidden, Disk-Based LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: The memory capacity required for large language models (LLMs) and their KV caches is a primary bottleneck for cost-effective, high-throughput serving. While prior work like Prism explores GPU sharing, and systems like vLLM manage KV cache memory, they primarily operate within DRAM constraints. Expanding the usable memory hierarchy to include NVMe and disk is critical for serving massive models or many concurrent sessions, but the high latency of disk access has made it impractical for interactive inference.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by co-designing a lightweight runtime scheduler with model architecture analysis to predict future parameter and KV cache accesses, we can orchestrate aggressive, asynchronous prefetching from disk into a faster buffer tier, completely hiding disk latency and making disk a viable, high-capacity tier for interactive LLM inference.
proposals.method: We will design a tiered memory manager (DRAM, NVMe, Disk) that orchestrates data movement based on real-time access predictions. The system will include: 1) a static analyzer to profile model layer dependencies and generate access patterns, 2) a lightweight runtime scheduler that, using the current decoding step and request context, predicts future block accesses (parameters and KV cache), and 3) a prefetching engine that asynchronously moves predicted blocks from disk/NVMe to DRAM ahead of compute. We will adapt scheduling fairness concepts from prior work to manage prefetching priorities across multiple requests.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel end-to-end system that demonstrably achieves interactive latencies for LLM inference using disk-backed memory, significantly reducing serving cost per token. It will also provide new techniques for predictive access modeling in transformer-based inference and a tiered orchestration policy that moves beyond LRU-based caching.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing caching systems that react to recent usage, our system proactively prefetches based on predictive models of future accesses, co-designed with the LLM architecture. This transforms the disk's role from a direct, latency-exposed data source to a background reservoir for a predictive loading system, a non-obvious shift given the prevailing focus on in-memory optimization.

proposals.title: TierRank: I/O-Aware Tournament Scheduling for Efficient Large-Scale Model Ranking
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of LLMs and specialized models has made large-scale comparative evaluation, as seen in platforms like Chatbot Arena, essential but extremely resource-intensive. Current methods for pairwise ranking tournaments load models into GPU memory ad-hoc, causing severe I/O bottlenecks when the model set exceeds RAM capacity, drastically slowing down interactive evaluation. A systems-aware approach is needed to make statistically rigorous, large-N model tournaments practically feasible.
proposals.hypothesis: By co-designing the tournament bracket structure with the storage tier's data layout and prefetching strategy, we can significantly reduce the total I/O cost and latency of a pairwise model ranking tournament, enabling interactive ranking of model sets an order of magnitude larger than available memory.
proposals.method: We will design TierRank, a system that treats model parameters as large blobs on disk. It will first cluster models based on storage locality (e.g., contiguous blocks). The scheduler will then use a modified Swiss-system or round-robin tournament algorithm that prioritizes pairwise comparisons between models within the same or adjacent storage clusters in each round. This allows for batched, sequential disk reads. We will integrate a prefetching buffer that loads the next cluster's models while the current comparisons are computed, overlapping I/O with GPU inference.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel system for efficient large-scale model ranking that bridges sports tournament theory and systems optimization. It will provide a publicly available scheduler and a cost model for I/O-aware tournament design, enabling researchers to run more comprehensive model evaluations with fixed hardware budgets.
proposals.novelty: While prior work like Prism optimizes GPU sharing for serving and fair scheduling allocates compute, no existing system co-optimizes the statistical efficiency of a tournament structure with the physical locality of models on secondary storage to minimize I/O for ranking.

proposals.title: Orion: A Locality-Aware, Prefetching Scheduler for Disk-Tiered Interactive LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: The memory footprint of large language models (LLMs) is a primary bottleneck for cost-effective, multi-model serving, as seen in systems like Prism. While disk offers vast, cheap storage, its high latency makes it unsuitable for interactive inference. Enabling viable disk-tiered inference would dramatically reduce serving costs and increase the number of concurrently hostable models, but requires overcoming orders-of-magnitude slower access speeds.
proposals.hypothesis: By co-designing a locality-aware inference scheduler with a predictive, block-level prefetcher, we can mask disk latency sufficiently to meet interactive response time targets (e.g., <200ms token latency) for a significant subset of requests, making disk a viable memory tier for warm, less-frequently accessed models in a multi-LLM cluster.
proposals.method: We will design Orion, a serving system that extends GPU sharing frameworks with a disk-tier manager. Orion will integrate: 1) A **locality-aware fair scheduler** that batches requests targeting the same disk-resident model and its specific activation blocks, minimizing disk seeks. 2) A **transformer-aware prefetching engine** that uses lightweight, online analysis of early decoder-layer attention patterns to predict and asynchronously load required KV cache blocks from disk for later layers. 3) A **selective caching policy** that pins frequently accessed attention heads in GPU memory, informed by access patterns observed in systems like Chatbot Arena.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel open-source serving system that demonstrably uses disk for interactive LLM inference, a new scheduling algorithm for I/O-bound model loading, and a quantitative analysis of the latency-cost trade-off frontier for multi-model serving. It directly advances the field of efficient LLM systems by expanding the viable memory hierarchy.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work using disk only for checkpoint loading or non-interactive batch processing, Orion targets interactive latency. Its novelty lies in the tight coupling of the inference scheduler with the prefetcher, using the computational graph's structure to drive I/O decisions, which is unexplored in pre-2026 LLM serving systems.

proposals.title: DeltaFlow: Dynamic, Model-Aware Offloading for Continuously Evolving LLMs
proposals.motivation: Current LLM serving systems, including GPU sharing frameworks like Prism, assume static models. However, real-world deployment increasingly involves continuous learning, such as online fine-tuning, lifelong adaptation, and frequent safety updates. This creates a critical systems bottleneck: naively swapping entire evolving models between GPU and CPU memory wastes bandwidth, increases latency, and disrupts serving fairness and throughput.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by tracking fine-grained model deltas (parameter changes, optimizer states, structural modifications) and integrating this metadata into the offloading scheduler, we can perform predictive and differential data movement that significantly reduces memory traffic and improves overall system efficiency for continuously learning LLMs.
proposals.method: We will design a runtime layer that instruments the training framework (e.g., PyTorch) to capture fine-grained write traces and model deltas. This metadata will feed a novel 'Delta-Aware Scheduler' that extends locality-aware scheduling principles to manage differential offloading, moving only changed components. We will develop lightweight predictors, using patterns from the learning trajectory, to pre-fetch likely future parameter states. The system will be integrated into a multi-LLM serving stack to evaluate its impact on throughput and fairness.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first offloading system designed for non-static LLMs, enabling efficient concurrent serving and continuous learning. It will provide a new abstraction—the model delta stream—for systems to reason about model evolution, directly improving memory utilization and bandwidth efficiency in lifelong learning scenarios.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on static model offloading or GPU sharing, DeltaFlow explicitly models and exploits the temporal locality and sparsity of changes within a learning model, transforming the offloading problem from whole-model swapping to intelligent differential data movement.

proposals.title: LibCache: Version-Aware Parameter Caching for Efficient Continuous Learning Model Serving
proposals.motivation: The trend towards continuous learning and fine-tuning of LLMs (e.g., via online RLHF or domain adaptation) creates a dynamic serving environment where models are frequently updated. Existing GPU memory management and offloading systems, like vLLM or PagedAttention, assume static model weights, leading to inefficient full-model transfers for each update. This incurs prohibitive communication and I/O overhead, especially for edge or multi-tenant GPU clusters, severely limiting the agility of deploying learned improvements.
proposals.hypothesis: By treating a dynamically updated LLM as a versioned software library and caching its parameter blocks with semantic versioning, we can serve multiple model versions with high memory efficiency and drastically reduce update bandwidth by transmitting only delta changes between versions.
proposals.method: We will design a caching layer that decomposes a model (e.g., a Transformer) into versioned blocks (e.g., per-layer parameter tensors). Each block is hashed, and a dependency graph tracks version compatibility. On an update, a diff algorithm (inspired by rsync or binary delta encoding) computes minimal deltas between version-hashed blocks. A resolver on the GPU, similar to a dynamic linker, reconstructs the target model version by fetching and applying deltas to cached blocks. We will integrate this with a scheduler (extending fairness-aware systems like Prism) to manage cache allocation and prefetching across multiple evolving models.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel caching abstraction and protocol for versioned model parameters, reducing update bandwidth by orders of magnitude for minor updates. 2) A system implementation that increases serving throughput and memory efficiency for multi-version, continuously learning LLMs in shared GPU environments. 3) Empirical evaluation demonstrating cost and latency benefits for realistic continuous learning workloads.
proposals.novelty: This work is the first to apply software library versioning and linking principles to the problem of dynamic model parameter management, moving beyond static weight offloading. It introduces semantic versioning and delta updates for model weights, a concept not explored in prior model serving systems focused on static graphs or fixed checkpoints.

proposals.title: TournamentLLM: Efficient Edge Offloading for Dynamic Model Populations via Optimized Pairwise Comparison
proposals.motivation: The rapid evolution of LLMs through fine-tuning and continuous learning creates dynamic model populations, making it costly to serve all versions centrally or distribute them naively to resource-constrained edges. Existing multi-model serving systems like Prism focus on GPU sharing for static sets, while evaluation platforms like Chatbot Arena are not designed for low-cost, automated model selection for deployment. A systematic method is needed to identify and offload only the best-performing model variants, minimizing both evaluation overhead and edge resource consumption.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that structuring the evaluation of a dynamic model set as an optimized knockout tournament, using minimal pairwise inference-based comparisons, can identify a high-performing elite subset with statistical confidence, enabling cost-optimal model offloading to edge devices.
proposals.method: We will design a framework that, upon new model versions, initiates a tournament. It uses a locality-aware scheduler (extending prior fairness work) to efficiently run pairwise comparisons on shared GPUs, using a distilled 'judge' model or human preference data from platforms like Arena to cast 'votes'. We will adapt tournament design algorithms from sports statistics (e.g., Swiss-system, double-elimination) to minimize total inference rounds while ensuring robust ranking. A decision module will then offload only top-ranked models to edges, using model sharing techniques to manage memory.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel tournament-based evaluation framework for dynamic model populations, 2) algorithms for minimizing inference cost in model comparison, and 3) an integrated system policy for cost-aware model offloading in edge-server hierarchies, directly reducing serving costs for continuously updated LLMs.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious fusion lies in applying structured tournament design—a concept from sports and statistics—to the systems problem of model selection and offloading, creating a new optimization point between evaluation accuracy and serving resource efficiency for dynamic models.

proposals.title: Dynamic Offloading for Continuous LLM Learning: A Memory-Aware Scheduling Framework
proposals.motivation: Current LLM serving systems, like Prism, optimize for static, pre-trained models, assuming stable GPU memory footprints. However, models increasingly undergo continuous learning (e.g., fine-tuning, RLHF) or frequent updates, causing dynamic and unpredictable memory demands. This creates severe interference with concurrent inference jobs, degrading throughput and violating the fairness guarantees studied in prior work.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a locality-aware, predictive offloading scheduler can decouple the volatile memory demands of continuous learning tasks from stable inference serving, maintaining high aggregate GPU utilization and system throughput while ensuring fairness.
proposals.method: We will design a scheduler that profiles the memory access patterns of learning tasks (e.g., optimizer states, gradient checkpoints) and inference queries. Using a predictive model of memory pressure, it will proactively offload specific, reusable components of the learning model (like certain adapter layers or optimizer partitions) to CPU or NVMe storage, guided by access locality. The scheduler will integrate with a system like vLLM or Prism, using a fairness-aware policy to allocate GPU memory slots dynamically between learning and inference, minimizing blocking.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel scheduling framework for mixed continuous-learning and inference workloads, 2) a set of locality-aware offloading policies for dynamic model components, and 3) an open-source system extension that demonstrates significantly higher throughput and better fairness compared to static serving baselines under model updates.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior GPU sharing work for static models, this explicitly targets the temporal memory volatility of learning tasks. It combines predictive offloading with fairness scheduling, a novel integration for the continuous learning setting.

proposals.title: EcoServe: A Constrained Optimization Framework for Energy-Efficient LLM Serving with Quality-of-Service Guarantees
proposals.motivation: The energy and computational cost of serving large language models (LLMs) is growing unsustainably. Current efficiency optimizations, such as dynamic batching, quantization, and speculative decoding, risk degrading response quality or violating latency Service Level Agreements (SLAs) for critical applications. A principled framework is needed to maximize efficiency while strictly enforcing application-defined effectiveness constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by modeling LLM serving as a constrained optimization problem—minimizing a system-wide efficiency metric (e.g., Joules per request) subject to per-request effectiveness bounds (e.g., a minimum Chatbot Arena ELO score or a maximum tail latency)—we can achieve significant resource savings without compromising usable performance.
proposals.method: We will design EcoServe, a co-optimization scheduler extending concepts from Prism and fair scheduling. It will treat precision levels (FP16/INT8), batch sizes, and decoding algorithms (e.g., speculative vs. autoregressive) as discrete levers. For each request with a specified effectiveness constraint (e.g., 'answer must be within 5 ELO of full-precision model'), a lightweight predictor will estimate the quality impact of each lever. EcoServe will then solve a per-time-window constrained optimization, using techniques like Lagrangian relaxation, to select configurations that minimize total cluster energy while satisfying all constraints.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel formulation of LLM serving as an effectiveness-constrained efficiency optimization problem. 2) The EcoServe prototype, demonstrating up to 40% energy reduction on mixed workloads while adhering to constraints. 3) A public trace dataset of LLM requests annotated with domain-specific effectiveness metrics to drive future research.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that trades off quality and efficiency in an unconstrained Pareto frontier, EcoServe enforces a hard, user-specified lower bound on effectiveness, reframing the goal as resource minimization within a region of *acceptable* solutions. This integrates quality-aware evaluation (from Chatbot Arena) directly into the scheduling loop.

proposals.title: EcoRouter: A/B Testing for Pareto-Optimal Efficiency in Multi-LLM Serving
proposals.motivation: The cost of serving large language models (LLMs) is dominated by inference-time computational resources, yet current serving systems and evaluation platforms like Chatbot Arena primarily optimize for latency/throughput or aggregate quality. There is no systematic framework to continuously measure and optimize for the total computational efficiency (e.g., energy, FLOPs) of different models across diverse, real-world query segments. This gap leads to static, over-provisioned deployments that waste resources without adapting to workload-specific efficiency-quality trade-offs.
proposals.hypothesis: By continuously A/B testing LLMs on live traffic with segment-specific efficiency metrics, we can build a routing system that dynamically selects a Pareto-optimal model for each query, significantly reducing total system cost (energy, FLOPs) while preserving tailored quality of service.
proposals.method: We will design EcoRouter, a serving system that extends GPU-sharing architectures like Prism. It integrates a continuous experimentation layer that runs concurrent, low-overhead A/B tests on sampled queries, collecting fine-grained metrics on task performance (e.g., win rate), computational cost (inferred energy, FLOPs), and latency. A multi-objective bandit algorithm, informed by locality-aware scheduling principles, will analyze these results to learn segment-specific Pareto frontiers. The router will then apply a cost-aware policy to select the most efficient model meeting a quality threshold for each incoming request.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel serving system that makes efficiency a first-class, optimizable metric for live LLM deployment. 2) A methodology for continuous, low-overhead efficiency A/B testing integrated into inference pipelines. 3) Empirical evidence on the Pareto-optimal efficiency-quality trade-offs for modern LLMs across diverse real-world workloads.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond static model comparisons and single-metric optimization (e.g., throughput) by framing efficiency as a dynamic, segment-specific target for adaptive routing, a synthesis not addressed by prior work on GPU sharing or fairness-aware scheduling alone.

proposals.title: FlowLLM: Credit-Based Flow Control for Adaptive GPU Offloading of Dynamic LLM Batches
proposals.motivation: Current GPU offloading frameworks for large language model (LLM) inference enforce uniform batch sizes to manage host-device memory transfers, leading to significant inefficiencies. In real-world serving systems like those studied in Prism and Chatbot Arena, request arrival is bursty and sequences have variable lengths, causing frequent batch padding, memory waste, and GPU underutilization. A system that can efficiently handle irregular, dynamic batches is crucial for improving throughput and cost-effectiveness in multi-LLM serving.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting TCP's credit-based flow control and dynamic buffering to the GPU offloading context will allow for efficient processing of variable-sized batches, eliminating padding overhead and improving GPU utilization without causing memory overflow or stalling.
proposals.method: We will design a credit-based flow control protocol between the CPU host and GPU device. The host will manage a pool of variable-sized memory buffers (slots) on the GPU, advertising available capacity via credits. An adaptive scheduler, inspired by locality-aware and fair scheduling principles, will pack incoming variable-length sequences into these buffers without padding. We will implement a prototype within an existing offloading framework (e.g., FlexGen or Petals), using a sliding-window mechanism to manage credits and buffer allocation, dynamically adjusting based on GPU compute feedback and batch completion signals.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel offloading mechanism that increases GPU utilization and throughput for dynamic workloads, reducing latency tail and improving cost-efficiency. It will provide a new systems abstraction for variable-batch offloading, advancing the state-of-the-art in memory- and throughput-constrained LLM serving.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the cross-domain analogy, applying TCP's mature flow-control semantics to solve the distinct but structurally similar problem of GPU memory bandwidth management for LLM inference, moving beyond static batching assumptions prevalent in pre-2026 offloading systems.

proposals.title: BatchBandit: Adaptive LLM Offloading via Online Experimentation with Variable-Sized Batches
proposals.motivation: LLM serving systems face a critical trade-off between cost, latency, and quality, especially with the rise of heterogeneous hardware (e.g., on-prem GPUs, cloud instances, specialized accelerators) and model variants (e.g., quantized, distilled). Existing offloading strategies are static or require offline profiling, failing to adapt to dynamic workloads and request characteristics. A system is needed to continuously and efficiently evaluate these trade-offs under real production traffic.
proposals.hypothesis: By framing variable-batch inference requests as a contextual bandit problem, we can build a system that performs continuous, low-overhead A/B testing to dynamically identify the optimal model-hardware pairing for different request segments, thereby optimizing a joint objective of cost and latency without compromising quality.
proposals.method: We will design a scheduler that treats each variable-sized request batch as a bandit 'pull'. The system will maintain multiple 'arms' representing different serving configurations (e.g., full model on GPU, offloaded layers on CPU, a distilled model). For each batch, segmented by features like average token count and user SLA, the scheduler will select an arm using a Thompson Sampling or UCB variant adapted for batch-level rewards (e.g., aggregate throughput/latency). Performance metrics are collected per batch, updating a Bayesian model to guide future routing. The core innovation is integrating batch-aware reward estimation and variance reduction techniques into the bandit algorithm.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel serving system that unifies dynamic batching with online experimentation for adaptive offloading, 2) a new bandit algorithm designed for variable-batch, low-frequency feedback environments, and 3) an open-source implementation and evaluation demonstrating significant cost savings and latency improvements over static and fairness-aware baselines like Prism.
proposals.novelty: While prior work like Prism shares GPUs and fairness-aware scheduling manages resources, no system treats the inherent variability in batch size and composition as a direct opportunity for robust, continuous online A/B testing to automate offloading decisions in real-time.

proposals.title: Vortex: Dynamic Batch Sizing for Heterogeneous LLM Inference with GPU Offloading
proposals.motivation: Modern LLM serving systems using GPU offloading (e.g., FlexGen, DeepSpeed-Zero-Inference) often assume uniform batch sizes across iterations to simplify memory management and scheduling. However, real-world serving workloads are bursty and heterogeneous, leading to inefficient GPU utilization and high latency when requests cannot be batched uniformly. This mismatch wastes the potential of offloading frameworks to maximize throughput under dynamic load.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a scheduler-aware offloading framework, which dynamically partitions and schedules variable-sized request batches, can significantly improve GPU utilization and reduce average job completion time (JCT) compared to systems enforcing batch uniformity.
proposals.method: We will design Vortex, a runtime system that integrates with an offloading-based inference engine. Vortex will: 1) Implement a dynamic memory planner that allocates GPU memory in variable-sized chunks per request, using a buddy-allocator-like scheme to minimize fragmentation. 2) Develop a cost model that predicts the latency of different batch partitionings, considering the data movement costs of offloading. 3) Create a scheduler that groups incoming requests of varying sizes into GPU execution waves to maximize throughput, drawing on concepts from locality-aware scheduling and multi-tenant GPU sharing (e.g., Prism).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic framework for efficient variable-batch inference in offloading-based LLM serving. We will release an open-source system that demonstrates substantial improvements in throughput and JCT on heterogeneous workloads, along with a new benchmark for evaluating dynamic batching in resource-constrained environments.
proposals.novelty: While prior work on batching (e.g., continuous batching) focuses on in-memory GPUs, and offloading work assumes uniform batches, Vortex uniquely tackles the co-design of dynamic batching and explicit memory movement for offloaded inference, a largely unexplored intersection.

proposals.title: ER-LLM: Active CPU-Side Preprocessing for Overlapped Memory-Compute in LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: In pre-2026 LLM serving, the high cost of GPU memory and its underutilization during token generation are critical bottlenecks. The CPU's role is largely passive, managing I/O and KV cache swapping, while the GPU waits on memory bandwidth. This wastes the CPU's available compute cycles and fails to address the latency and throughput penalties of memory-bound generation steps.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by redesigning the CPU's role to perform active, lightweight computations on data in-flight from main memory to the GPU, we can significantly overlap memory access with useful preprocessing, reducing the computational burden on the accelerator and improving overall system throughput and latency.
proposals.method: We will design a system where CPU cores, or dedicated units on the CPU die, intercept and process data streams (e.g., KV cache blocks, attention scores, or logit vectors) during GPU generation. We will implement and evaluate specific algorithms for this co-processor, such as dynamic sparse attention pruning based on runtime score distributions, data layout reorganization for more coalesced GPU memory accesses, and lightweight preconditioning of activation vectors. The system will be integrated into a modified vLLM or similar serving framework, with careful scheduling to avoid adding CPU-side critical path latency.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel system architecture for LLM inference that actively utilizes CPU compute to ameliorate GPU memory bottlenecks. It will provide a new design principle for heterogeneous computing in AI serving and deliver open-source implementations demonstrating measurable improvements in tokens/sec/dollar and latency for large-batch, multi-LLM serving scenarios.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in treating the CPU as an active computational co-processor during the GPU's generation phase, not just a I/O manager. It moves beyond static offloading or swapping schemes (like FlexGen, DeepSpeed) by performing dynamic, data-dependent preprocessing concurrent with data movement, a concept unexplored in pre-2026 LLM systems.

proposals.title: ProbeGen: An Active CPU-Guided Framework for Adaptive and Discriminative LLM Benchmarking
proposals.motivation: Current LLM benchmarks are static, offering a fixed snapshot of performance that fails to adaptively probe model strengths and weaknesses. This limits our ability to perform fine-grained, comparative diagnosis of model architectures, especially as systems research focuses on efficient multi-model serving. An adaptive, diagnostic benchmarking tool is needed to uncover the fundamental computational roles of different model components and inform better serving system design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an active CPU agent, using real-time feedback from model performance on initial probes, can dynamically generate increasingly discriminative questions that stress specific model capabilities, thereby creating a more efficient and insightful benchmarking loop than static datasets.
proposals.method: We will design a closed-loop system where a lightweight CPU-based orchestrator runs a meta-algorithm (e.g., a bandit or evolutionary strategy) on performance metrics from a target LLM cluster. This orchestrator will guide a separate, cost-effective 'probe-generator' LLM (e.g., a 7B parameter model) to produce new test questions. The CPU agent will analyze correctness, latency, and confidence scores from the target models to formulate prompts for the generator, focusing on underperforming domains like long-context reasoning or multi-step factual chains. The system will be integrated with a serving platform like vLLM for efficient inference.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, adaptive benchmarking framework that generates harder, more diagnostic questions over time, 2) a methodology to quantitatively link specific question types to model architectural choices (e.g., attention mechanisms, MoE layers), and 3) open-sourced probe datasets and the orchestrator code to advance evaluation beyond static leaderboards.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in transforming the CPU from a passive memory/coordination unit into an active, diagnostic computation agent for benchmarking, creating a synergistic feedback loop between system-level performance monitoring and algorithmic question generation that does not exist in static benchmarks like MMLU or Chatbot Arena.

proposals.title: CPU-Accelerated Drafting: Offloading Speculative Decoding Logic to the Host for Higher LLM Inference Throughput
proposals.motivation: Current LLM serving systems treat the CPU as a passive memory manager, primarily handling KV cache I/O and request scheduling. This underutilizes abundant CPU cycles, especially in multi-tenant GPU clusters. As models and context lengths grow, the memory and compute pressure on GPUs becomes the primary bottleneck for throughput and cost.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that strategically offloading parts of the speculative decoding pipeline—specifically the drafting model execution and verification logic—to the CPU can increase overall system throughput by reducing GPU contention and better leveraging heterogeneous resources.
proposals.method: We will design a system that partitions the speculative decoding workflow. A small, efficient draft model (e.g., a distilled version of the target LLM) will run on the CPU, generating candidate token sequences. The CPU will also manage the initial verification step, preparing data for the GPU's final, parallel scoring of the draft. We will develop a locality-aware scheduler (extending prior fairness work) to coordinate this CPU-GPU pipeline, minimizing data movement and synchronizing batch processing for the target LLM on the GPU.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel serving architecture that actively uses CPU compute to amplify GPU efficiency, a system prototype demonstrating measurable throughput gains, and a scheduling policy for fair, efficient CPU-assisted speculative execution in multi-model settings.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses the CPU for KV cache management (e.g., via SSD offloading) and scheduling, we propose a first-principles redesign of the speculative decoding algorithm itself to be a heterogeneous CPU-GPU task, moving active computation traditionally done on the GPU to the host.

proposals.title: JITO: Just-In-Time Offloading for Adaptive Low-Latency LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: Current offloading systems for large language models (LLMs), like FlexGen, prioritize high-throughput batch processing, making them ill-suited for interactive, low-latency applications. As LLMs are increasingly deployed in conversational agents and real-time tools, there is a critical need for offloading engines that can dynamically balance immediate responsiveness with long-term efficiency. This gap limits the cost-effective deployment of large models in latency-sensitive user-facing services.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a JIT-inspired offloading scheduler, which profiles inference patterns and caches optimized execution plans for stable workloads, can achieve near-instantaneous initial response times while asymptotically approaching the high throughput of batch-optimized systems.
proposals.method: We will design a two-phase scheduler. The 'Interpreter' phase executes initial requests using a conservative, latency-first strategy that minimizes GPU-CPU data movement. Concurrently, a profiler traces the computational graph and request sequences. For identified hot paths (e.g., repeated prompts or model layers), a 'Compiler' phase generates optimized, throughput-oriented offloading plans using a cost model, which are memoized. A lightweight runtime monitors workload stability to dynamically switch between cached plans and the interpreter.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel offloading system that unifies low-latency and high-throughput objectives, enabling cost-efficient interactive LLM serving on resource-constrained hardware. It will contribute a formal analogy between JIT compilation and offloading scheduling, alongside a practical open-source implementation evaluated on real-world chat workloads.
proposals.novelty: While prior work like Prism optimizes GPU sharing for multi-model throughput and FlexGen optimizes for batch throughput, JITO is the first to adapt the core JIT compilation paradigm—profiling and caching hot paths—specifically to bridge the latency-throughput trade-off in single-model offloading for interactive serving.

proposals.title: ProbeGen: Adaptive Offloading for Interactive LLMs via Automated Performance Discrimination
proposals.motivation: Offloading is a key technique for serving large language models (LLMs) on resource-constrained hardware, but existing systems like FlexGen are optimized for static, high-throughput batch workloads. Interactive applications, such as chatbots, demand low and predictable latency, which is highly sensitive to the dynamic interplay between model architecture, hardware characteristics (e.g., PCIe bandwidth), and offloading parameters. A one-size-fits-all configuration fails to achieve optimal interactive performance across diverse models and hardware setups.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a closed-loop system, which uses an adaptive question generator to create low-latency probes that discriminate specific hardware bottlenecks, can dynamically infer optimal offloading parameters (e.g., chunk size, prefetch depth) for a given model-hardware pair, thereby minimizing latency for interactive queries.
proposals.method: We will build a system integrating a modified FlexGen offloading engine with a novel adaptive probe generator. The generator, inspired by techniques for model evaluation and discrimination, will produce minimal token sequences designed to stress specific subsystems (e.g., memory bandwidth via long contexts, compute via complex operations). A lightweight profiler will measure the latency of these probes. Using Bayesian optimization, the system will analyze probe results to form a performance model and iteratively adjust the offloading scheduler's parameters in real-time, creating a tailored configuration for interactive workloads.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel methodology for automatically characterizing the latency profile of an offloaded LLM, 2) a closed-loop, adaptive offloading scheduler that optimizes for interactive latency rather than aggregate throughput, and 3) an open-source system demonstrating significant latency reduction for interactive queries compared to static offloading configurations in pre-2026 systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., Prism) optimizes GPU sharing for throughput and fairness, and FlexGen focuses on offline batching, our approach is the first to close the loop by using *generated diagnostic probes* to dynamically configure offloading for *interactive latency*, a distinct and critical optimization target.

proposals.title: FlexGen-LS: A Low-Latency Offloading Engine for Interactive LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: The high cost of GPU memory for large language models (LLMs) necessitates offloading parameters to CPU/disk, as pioneered by systems like FlexGen. However, FlexGen and similar high-throughput offloading engines prioritize aggregate batch throughput, resulting in high per-request latency unsuitable for interactive applications like chatbots. As LLMs move into latency-sensitive user-facing services, there is a critical need for cost-efficient offloading that also meets strict tail-latency service-level objectives (SLOs).
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by redesigning FlexGen's scheduling and offloading strategy to prioritize latency-critical requests and exploit finer-grained, predictive data movement, we can achieve interactive latencies (e.g., < 1 second for first token) while maintaining high GPU utilization and cost efficiency through resource sharing.
proposals.method: We will develop a new scheduler that integrates preemption and priority queues, inspired by fairness-aware scheduling from recent work, to isolate latency-sensitive requests. We will augment the offloading engine with a locality-aware, block-level prefetcher that predicts and stages upcoming transformer layers into GPU memory based on real-time generation progress. The system will employ a dynamic batching policy that segregates interactive and batch requests, using selective offloading and GPU memory partitioning to minimize interference.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first open-source offloading engine capable of serving LLMs interactively on resource-constrained GPUs. It will provide a systematic evaluation of the latency-throughput trade-off in offloaded inference and establish new design principles for cost-effective, low-latency LLM serving.
proposals.novelty: While prior work on FlexGen optimized for throughput and recent scheduling research improved fairness, combining predictive, fine-grained offloading with priority-based scheduling for latency-sensitive LLM tasks is a novel systems challenge. This approach moves beyond treating offloading as a batch processing problem.

proposals.title: TASC: Task-Aware, Differentiable Co-Search for Accuracy-Constrained LLM Compression
proposals.motivation: Deploying large language models (LLMs) is constrained by high memory and bandwidth demands for both weights and the KV cache, limiting throughput and increasing serving costs. Existing compression techniques, like post-training quantization, often treat weight and cache compression separately and rely on generic heuristics (e.g., perplexity) that do not guarantee performance on a user's specific end-task, such as chat or code generation. A systematic, automated method to find a Pareto-optimal compression policy under a strict task-level accuracy constraint is urgently needed for cost-efficient, high-quality LLM serving.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a joint, differentiable search over mixed-precision configurations for both weights and the KV cache, guided by lightweight surrogate models trained to predict task-specific accuracy, can discover compression policies that strictly satisfy user-specified accuracy constraints while maximizing memory and throughput gains.
proposals.method: We propose a bilevel optimization framework. The inner loop trains fast, parameterized surrogate models (e.g., small neural networks or gradient-boosted trees) on a sampled set of (compression_config, task_accuracy) pairs to predict accuracy for any configuration. The outer loop performs constrained optimization using a differentiable relaxation of the discrete search space (inspired by DARTS) to maximize a compression reward (e.g., memory reduction, expected speedup) subject to the surrogate-predicted accuracy being above a user-defined threshold. The search directly incorporates system-level costs, integrating profiling data akin to Prism's sharing-aware scheduling.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver 1) an automated system for generating task-aware, accuracy-guaranteed LLM compression policies, 2) a novel constrained co-search formulation that unifies weight and KV cache compression, and 3) empirical validation on diverse tasks (e.g., from Chatbot Arena) showing superior compression-performance trade-offs compared to static or task-agnostic baselines.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that compresses weights and cache independently or optimizes for generic metrics, TASC performs a joint, differentiable search directly constrained by end-task accuracy, using learned surrogates to make the expensive evaluation of configurations tractable.

proposals.title: SparqSwap: Direct Execution of Compressed Weights for Seamless GPU Offloading in LLM Serving
proposals.motivation: The memory footprint of large language models (LLMs) is a primary bottleneck for high-throughput, multi-model serving, as seen in systems like Prism. While weight offloading and compression (e.g., quantization, sparsity) are established techniques, their benefits are often negated by the latency and compute overhead of decompressing weights back to a dense, native format before execution. This decompression tax limits the practical gains from memory savings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by co-designing a block-wise sparse-quantized weight format with specialized GPU kernels and an offloading scheduler aware of this format, we can eliminate decompression overhead, allowing memory savings from compression to translate directly into improved end-to-end latency and throughput for multi-LLM inference.
proposals.method: We will design a fixed, hardware-friendly block-sparse quantized format where weights are organized in predictable, aligned blocks for direct memory mapping. We will implement a suite of specialized GPU kernels that perform matrix multiplications directly on this compressed format using sparse, low-precision arithmetic. The offloading scheduler, extending locality-aware principles, will manage the fetching and swapping of these compressed blocks between GPU and CPU memory, scheduling them for execution by the corresponding specialized kernels without an intermediate decompression stage.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel end-to-end system for compressed weight offloading that eliminates decompression overhead, 2) a set of directly executable, block-wise sparse-quantized formats and their associated high-performance kernels, and 3) an empirical demonstration that this co-design significantly improves the cost-efficiency and throughput of serving multiple LLMs compared to state-of-the-art offloading systems.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the tight integration of the compression format, execution kernels, and offloading scheduler to enable direct execution, moving beyond the conventional 'compress-store-decompress-execute' pipeline. This makes the offloading of compressed weights truly seamless for the first time.

proposals.title: SpectraServe: Hierarchical, Loss-Aware Compression for Memory-Efficient LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: The high memory footprint of large language models (LLMs) necessitates offloading weights to CPU or disk, creating a severe memory-throughput bottleneck as in systems like FlexGen. Current compression techniques like uniform quantization treat all parameters equally, missing opportunities for greater efficiency. A co-designed approach that stratifies parameter importance and aligns it with the memory hierarchy could dramatically improve inference throughput for memory-constrained serving.
proposals.hypothesis: Transforming LLM weights into a learned basis to stratify their task-critical importance, and then applying aggressive, lossy compression to less important components specifically when they reside in offloaded memory, will enable higher compression ratios with minimal accuracy loss compared to uniform methods, thereby improving overall serving throughput.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-phase method. First, we adapt a lightweight, learned transform (e.g., a low-rank autoencoder) to project model weights into a basis where dimensions are ranked by their measured sensitivity to output accuracy via gradient-based heuristics. Second, we integrate this with an offloading scheduler, applying ultra-low-bit quantization or structured pruning to the low-sensitivity components stored in slow memory, while keeping high-sensitivity components in GPU memory at high precision. The system will dynamically manage this hybrid representation during inference.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new paradigm of hierarchical, loss-aware compression co-designed with memory systems for LLM serving. It will provide a practical system, SpectraServe, that increases serving throughput under memory constraints. We will also deliver a theoretical and empirical analysis of weight sensitivity in transformed spaces.
proposals.novelty: Unlike uniform compression, our method explicitly creates a multi-resolution weight representation aligned with the memory hierarchy, inspired by signal processing codecs. This integration of lossy, importance-stratified compression with offloading scheduling is a novel systems-optimization approach not explored in prior LLM serving work.

proposals.title: CompServe: Adaptive, Real-Time Compression Tuning for Live A/B Testing of LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: Deploying large language models (LLMs) is costly due to high memory and compute demands. While numerous compression techniques (e.g., quantization, sparsity) exist, their optimal configuration is workload-dependent and typically evaluated in static, offline settings, which fails to capture real production dynamics. A system is needed to empirically determine the best compression strategy under live, fluctuating query loads to optimize cost-performance trade-offs continuously.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating real-time, multi-variant compression into a live A/B testing framework will enable automated, data-driven discovery of optimal compression configurations that outperform static choices, improving aggregate metrics like cost-normalized throughput or latency-accuracy Pareto frontiers for LLM serving.
proposals.method: We will build a serving system that dynamically instantiates multiple compressed model variants (e.g., using GPTQ/LLM.int8() for quantization, structured sparsity patterns, or FlexGen-inspired offloading) within a single GPU cluster, leveraging GPU sharing techniques akin to Prism. An adaptive scheduler, extending locality-aware fairness principles, will route identical production query streams to these variants for direct comparison. The system will continuously collect fine-grained telemetry on per-variant throughput, latency, hardware utilization, and accuracy (using lightweight proxies like output log-likelihood or embedding similarity) to perform real-time multi-armed bandit optimization for configuration selection.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel serving system that closes the loop between offline compression research and online deployment, enabling automated, empirical optimization of LLM compression. It will provide a framework and public benchmarks showing how optimal compression strategies shift with real-world workload characteristics, advancing the field of efficient LLM inference.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that evaluates compression offline or serves a single static model, this proposal fuses adaptive compression with live A/B testing, allowing continuous, in-production optimization of the compression-configuration space based on direct observation of system and quality metrics.

proposals.title: Archon: An Architecture-Aware Optimization Framework for Efficient LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of diverse LLM architectures (e.g., MoEs, SSMs) strains GPU memory and limits throughput in multi-model serving systems. Existing tensor placement and compression strategies are often designed for and transferred naively from Transformer-based models, leading to suboptimal performance on these emerging architectures due to their distinct computational graphs and memory access patterns. A principled, architecture-aware optimization framework is needed to unlock efficient resource utilization across the growing model zoo.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formally modeling the unique dataflow and dependency graphs of non-Transformer architectures, we can automatically derive near-optimal tensor placement and compression strategies that significantly outperform transferred Transformer-based heuristics in memory efficiency and inference throughput.
proposals.method: We will first develop a formal graph abstraction that captures operator dependencies, dataflow, and memory access patterns for target architectures (SSMs, MoEs, RNNs). Using this model, we will build a hybrid optimizer combining analytical cost modeling for rapid pruning and a Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) algorithm to explore the strategy space. The optimizer will co-optimize tensor placement (using a sharing-aware scheduler inspired by Prism) and selective quantization, with validation via a cycle-accurate simulator before deployment on a real serving system.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a formal, extensible graph model for non-Transformer LLM architectures, 2) an open-source optimization framework (Archon) that automatically generates architecture-specific tensor strategies, and 3) a comprehensive evaluation demonstrating substantial improvements in memory footprint and throughput for SSM and MoE models in multi-LLM serving scenarios.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that applies static optimizations, Archon automatically derives strategies from first principles tailored to each architecture's graph. It moves beyond Transformer-centric systems and scheduler-level fairness (like Locality-aware Fair Scheduling) to co-optimize low-level tensor placement and compression across diverse model families.

proposals.title: AMR-Tensor: Adaptive Mesh Refinement for Architecture-Agnostic Model Serving
proposals.motivation: Efficient serving of large models is dominated by Transformer-specific optimizations, leaving emerging architectures like State Space Models (SSMs) and Mixture of Experts (MoEs) under-optimized. These models have heterogeneous computational graphs and activation patterns, making uniform tensor compression or placement strategies suboptimal. A general, adaptive optimization framework is needed to improve memory and throughput for the next generation of non-Transformer models.
proposals.hypothesis: By applying principles from Computational Fluid Dynamics' Adaptive Mesh Refinement (AMR) to model computational graphs, we can create a non-uniform, dynamic tensor optimization strategy that significantly improves memory efficiency and inference throughput for diverse model architectures without sacrificing accuracy.
proposals.method: We will develop a profiler to analyze the computational graph and activation variance of target models (e.g., Mamba, MoE) during a calibration phase. Inspired by AMR, we will design a scheduler that partitions tensors into 'regions' and applies a policy—using metrics like sensitivity and access frequency—to select optimization primitives (e.g., NF4 vs. FP8 quantization, CPU offloading, recomputation). This policy will be integrated into a serving system prototype, extending prior work on fair and efficient GPU sharing, to dynamically adjust strategies per request and model region.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver 1) a novel, architecture-agnostic framework for adaptive tensor optimization, 2) a set of concrete policies for SSMs and MoEs, and 3) an open-source serving system integration demonstrating substantial improvements in cost-per-query and throughput compared to static baselines, broadening the scope of efficient LLM serving.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the cross-disciplinary analogy translating CFD's mesh refinement to tensor optimization, creating a dynamic, non-uniform strategy tailored to any model's computational flow, unlike static, Transformer-centric methods.

proposals.title: Mimir: Co-Designed Lightweight Sequence Models for Real-Time, On-Device Vote Manipulation Detection
proposals.motivation: The detection of sophisticated vote manipulation in online platforms is critical for trust, but current methods rely on centralized, high-latency analysis using large transformer models, which is neither scalable nor privacy-preserving. The rapid growth of edge AI and the need for low-latency, efficient inference on resource-constrained hardware (e.g., edge servers, mobile devices) demands a new class of models that can perform complex sequence analysis locally.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by co-designing novel, efficient sequence model architectures (like State Space Models or gated MLPs) with adversarial detection heads and optimizing them via advanced tensor placement and compression, we can achieve real-time, high-accuracy manipulation detection on resource-constrained hardware, surpassing the efficiency-accuracy trade-offs of adapted transformer models.
proposals.method: We will develop a serving system, Mimir, that first designs a core architecture-agnostic, lightweight model (e.g., based on Mamba or a gated MLP-Mixer) for temporal pattern recognition in user behavior sequences. An adversarial detection head will be intrinsically co-designed and trained with this backbone. We will then apply pre-2026 tensor compression techniques (e.g., structured pruning, low-rank factorization) and implement a locality-aware tensor placement scheduler—inspired by GPU sharing systems like Prism—to optimize memory and throughput for mixed CPU/GPU edge environments, enabling real-time inference.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a new class of efficient, trustworthy guardrail models for distributed systems, moving manipulation detection from the cloud to the edge; 2) a systematic framework for co-designing efficient backbones with adversarial tasks; and 3) an open-source serving system demonstrating real-time performance on commodity edge hardware, validated using benchmarks derived from platforms like Chatbot Arena.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the intrinsic co-design of non-transformer efficient sequence models specifically for an adversarial detection task, combined with a systems-driven optimization for edge deployment, moving beyond simply compressing existing large models or applying fairness-aware scheduling to transformer-based backends.

proposals.title: ArchFlex: Generalized Memory-Aware Scheduling and Compression for Non-Transformer Large Models
proposals.motivation: The explosive growth of large language models (LLMs) has driven significant systems research into efficient serving, primarily focused on Transformer architectures. However, emerging large-scale models based on alternative architectures (e.g., State Space Models like Mamba, or hybrid models) are gaining prominence for their efficiency and performance on long sequences. Pre-2026, the serving infrastructure and optimization techniques (like tensor placement, GPU sharing, and compression) are highly specialized for Transformers, creating a gap for efficiently deploying these diverse, next-generation model families at scale.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the core systems principles from Transformer-serving research—specifically dynamic tensor placement and selective activation compression—can be abstracted into architecture-agnostic primitives, enabling significant memory and throughput improvements for a broader class of large, autoregressive models.
proposals.method: We will first profile the memory access patterns and computational graphs of non-Transformer architectures (e.g., Mamba, RWKV) to identify key tensors and bottlenecks. We will then extend the locality-aware scheduling from 'Prism' and 'Locality-aware Fair Scheduling' by developing a generalized cost model for inter-operator dependencies and memory footprints across architectures. For compression, we will adapt activation checkpointing and quantization strategies to target the unique recurrent or stateful structures in these models, implementing a prototype scheduler within a modified vLLM or FlexGen serving system.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic framework for efficient serving of non-Transformer large models, reducing deployment costs. It will yield open-source, extensible scheduler and compression modules that support multiple model families. The evaluation, inspired by the scalable benchmarking of 'Chatbot Arena', will offer comparative performance insights across architectural paradigms.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes serving for a single architecture family (Transformers), this proposal generalizes these methods, creating a unified system for diverse, state-of-the-art model backends. The abstraction of placement and compression policies beyond the attention mechanism is a novel and necessary direction for sustainable AI serving.

proposals.title: FlowServe: A Streaming Dataflow Engine for High-Throughput, Low-Latency Multi-LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: The explosive growth of LLM applications has created a critical need for serving systems that maximize GPU utilization and throughput while meeting stringent latency SLOs. Current batched execution systems (e.g., vLLM, Orca) often treat request processing as monolithic kernels, leading to underutilization during I/O-bound phases like prompt loading and token generation. This inefficiency is exacerbated in multi-model serving scenarios, where diverse computational and memory profiles create complex scheduling challenges.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that reframing LLM inference as a fine-grained, asynchronous dataflow graph—where token generation, KV cache operations, and model computations are decoupled into parallelizable stages—can significantly improve hardware utilization and throughput by overlapping compute and I/O, analogous to pipelining in distributed data processing engines.
proposals.method: We will design FlowServe, a novel serving system that decomposes LLM inference (prefill and decode) into a DAG of operators (e.g., Attention, FFN, Sampling, KV Cache Read/Write). Inspired by Spark Streaming and Naiad, we will implement a locality-aware, fair scheduler that dynamically pipelines these operators across requests and models, treating GPU streams and HBM as first-class resources. Key algorithms will include a window-based operator scheduler for inter-request pipelining and a memory-aware operator placement policy to minimize data movement, building upon the fairness and sharing principles from the researcher's prior work (Prism, Locality-aware Fair Scheduling).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a new systems abstraction for LLM inference as a fine-grained dataflow, 2) the FlowServe prototype demonstrating superior throughput and latency compared to state-of-the-art batched servers, and 3) a quantitative analysis of the trade-offs between pipelining granularity, scheduling overhead, and hardware utilization in a multi-LLM setting.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes batching or introduces model parallelism, applying streaming dataflow engine principles to intra-request, operator-level execution for LLM serving is a novel systems direction. It moves beyond treating a model as a single kernel, enabling a new degree of overlap and scheduling flexibility.

proposals.title: TensorShatter: Optimizing LLM Inference Through Fine-Grained, Locality-Aware Tensor Placement Across Memory-Constrained Multi-GPU Nodes
proposals.motivation: The deployment of large language models (LLMs) is increasingly constrained by GPU memory, limiting batch sizes and throughput. While model parallelism and offloading exist, they are often coarse-grained, leading to significant inter-GPU communication overhead and underutilization of aggregate memory in nodes with multiple smaller GPUs (e.g., consumer-grade or older data center cards). An efficient, fine-grained method to partition and place model tensors across such a memory hierarchy is a critical, unsolved systems challenge for cost-effective serving.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a fine-grained, dynamic tensor placement strategy, informed by real-time access patterns and inter-tensor dependencies, can significantly outperform existing coarse-grained parallelism and offloading schemes by minimizing data movement and maximizing aggregate GPU memory utilization in multi-GPU, memory-constrained nodes.
proposals.method: We will design TensorShatter, a runtime system that profiles the memory access graph of an LLM during inference. It will employ a cost model weighing tensor size, access frequency, and producer-consumer relationships to algorithmically shard and place tensors across GPUs, extending concepts from Prism's sharing and locality-aware scheduling. We will implement a lightweight, asynchronous communication layer for necessary cross-GPU tensor transfers, integrated with a modified KV cache management policy to handle sequential decoding. The scheduler will be fairness-aware to manage multiple concurrent model requests.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel fine-grained tensor placement algorithm and runtime for multi-GPU LLM inference, 2) an open-source implementation demonstrating higher throughput and lower latency compared to pipeline/model parallelism and CPU offloading baselines on memory-constrained hardware, and 3) a characterization of the trade-offs between communication overhead and memory aggregation for modern LLM workloads.
proposals.novelty: Unlike coarse-grained model parallelism or simple offloading, TensorShatter performs sub-layer, inter-operator tensor partitioning and placement. Its novelty lies in the synthesis of a fine-grained, dataflow-aware placement optimizer with a locality- and fairness-aware multi-LLM scheduler, specifically targeting the under-explored regime of nodes with multiple small-memory GPUs.

proposals.title: Dual: A Bilevel Optimization Framework for Minimal-Cost LLM Serving with Guaranteed Throughput
proposals.motivation: The operational cost of large language model (LLM) serving is dominated by GPU hardware, yet current systems like vLLM and Orca are designed to maximize throughput for a fixed cluster. For cloud providers and research labs, the inverse problem is critical: given a target aggregate throughput (e.g., requests per second), what is the minimal hardware configuration to achieve it? Pre-2026 work on GPU sharing (Prism) and scheduling (Locality-aware Fair Scheduling) optimizes utilization but does not provide a predictive model for minimal cost procurement and deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formulating LLM serving as a bilevel optimization problem—where an outer loop searches the hardware configuration space and an inner loop simulates optimal scheduling—we can automatically identify Pareto-optimal (cost, throughput) operating points and guarantee a target throughput with minimal hardware.
proposals.method: We will develop Dual, a modeling and scheduling framework. First, we will create a cost-performance model for heterogeneous GPU nodes (memory, compute) based on pre-2026 LLM inference characteristics (memory-bound, KV cache). Second, we design a bilevel optimizer: the outer level uses Bayesian optimization to propose hardware configurations (GPU types/counts, memory); the inner level employs a novel, simulated scheduler extending Prism's sharing and Locality-aware Fair Scheduling to evaluate if the configuration meets the target throughput. The scheduler will incorporate a penalty for fairness violations to ensure QoS.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel optimization framework for minimal-cost LLM serving cluster design. 2) An open-source tool that, given a set of LLMs and a throughput SLO, outputs recommended hardware specs and a scheduling policy. 3) A comprehensive analysis of the trade-offs between GPU sharing granularity, fairness, and hardware cost for multi-model serving.
proposals.novelty: This work inverts the standard throughput-maximization objective and combines hardware configuration search with QoS-aware scheduling simulation, a synthesis not explored in pre-2026 systems like Prism or vLLM, which take hardware as a given.

proposals.title: Inverse: A System for Determining the Maximum LLM Size for a Target Throughput on Constrained Hardware
proposals.motivation: As LLMs grow, deploying them on fixed, often limited, hardware (e.g., a single GPU server) becomes a critical challenge for cost-effective and accessible inference. Existing serving systems like vLLM and Orca focus on maximizing throughput for a given model, but practitioners lack a principled method to answer the inverse question: what is the largest, most capable model that can run on my hardware while meeting a minimum usable throughput? This problem is central to making efficient use of existing infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by co-designing a predictive performance model with a novel, locality-aware scheduling and memory management strategy, we can systematically determine and serve a model significantly larger than what naive static partitioning allows, while maintaining a predefined minimum throughput target.
proposals.method: We will develop an analytical model that predicts the memory footprint and computation time of transformer layers under various partitioning schemes (tensor, pipeline, and expert parallelism) and KV cache management policies. Building on concepts from Prism and locality-aware scheduling, we will design a runtime system that dynamically partitions the model and schedules requests to maximize layer and attention cache locality within a single multi-GPU node. The system will use an iterative search algorithm, guided by the performance model, to find the maximum model parameters (e.g., by selecting a sub-network from a mixture-of-experts model or configuring parallelism) that satisfy a user-specified minimum queries-per-second.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel system, Inverse, that solves the model-maximization problem for fixed hardware, 2) a predictive performance model for LLM inference under complex parallelism, and 3) an open-source implementation and evaluation demonstrating practical model size increases on commodity servers while meeting throughput Service Level Objectives (SLOs).
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes throughput for a fixed model, Inverse inverts the problem to maximize model size for a fixed throughput target, requiring a new co-design of performance prediction and adaptive runtime scheduling. This approach is distinct from simple model compression or offloading, as it actively leverages parallelism and scheduling within hardware constraints.

proposals.title: PrefSpace: Learning Latent Preference Embeddings for Cost-Aware LLM Recommendation
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of large language models (LLMs) with diverse capabilities and costs makes model selection for a given user task a critical challenge. Current evaluation platforms like Chatbot Arena provide broad rankings but are expensive to scale and do not personalize recommendations based on user-specific constraints like latency, cost, or skill-specific accuracy. A scalable, data-efficient method is needed to map models and task contexts into a shared space for efficient retrieval.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a low-dimensional latent space can be learned from a sparse set of benchmark evaluations, where the geometric proximity between a user-task embedding and model embeddings predicts personalized performance ranking, enabling accurate recommendation without exhaustive evaluation.
proposals.method: We will design a matrix factorization system where rows are models and columns are task-context tuples (e.g., 'code generation, low-cost'). The observed matrix entries are performance scores (e.g., accuracy, normalized by cost). We will extend this with a two-tower neural architecture: one tower embeds model performance profiles, and the other embeds user-task requirement vectors. The model will be trained via a pairwise ranking loss (e.g., Bayesian Personalized Ranking) on data from platforms like Chatbot Arena, augmented with synthetic cost-aware constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, scalable system for personalized LLM recommendation that directly optimizes for user-specified constraints like cost and latency. It will provide a practical tool for efficient model serving selection, reducing the need for massive evaluation campaigns. The learned latent space will also offer interpretable insights into the trade-offs between different model families.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on fair scheduling or sharing for serving, this approach proactively learns a predictive preference space for recommendation, moving beyond static benchmarks. It combines collaborative filtering techniques from recommender systems with the unique constraints of LLM inference (cost, memory) in a novel way not explored in existing model evaluation literature.

proposals.title: Cerebro: CPU-Concurrent Online Learning for Real-Time LLM Selection and Throughput Optimization
proposals.motivation: Multi-LLM serving systems like Prism and evaluation platforms like Chatbot Arena highlight the need for dynamic model selection based on live performance. However, current systems treat ranking as a static or batch process, creating a bottleneck where model routing decisions lag behind real-time feedback. This inefficiency reduces system throughput and user-perceived quality, especially as model pools grow and performance drifts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that offloading the continuous online learning of competitive model rankings (e.g., Elo, TrueSkill) to the CPU as a concurrent agent will enable near-instantaneous, feedback-aware routing decisions without blocking GPU inference, thereby improving overall system throughput and the quality of served responses.
proposals.method: We will design a system architecture where a CPU-hosted agent runs lightweight online learning algorithms (e.g., online gradient descent for Bradley-Terry models, or contextual bandits) on streams of pairwise comparison feedback. This agent updates skill estimates in shared memory (inspired by locality-aware scheduling) concurrently with GPU text generation. The updated rankings immediately inform a scheduler that routes the next request to the currently best-performing model, integrating with a GPU-sharing backend like Prism for efficient resource utilization.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel system design that tightly couples concurrent CPU-based learning with GPU inference for adaptive LLM serving, 2) an open-source implementation demonstrating significant improvements in throughput and win-rate over static routing baselines, and 3) a formal analysis of the latency/accuracy trade-offs in online ranking for systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work separates inference scheduling from offline evaluation, our proposal uniquely transforms the CPU into an active, concurrent learning agent that updates model rankings in real-time, enabling a closed-loop, adaptive serving system previously unexplored.

proposals.title: Dynamic Discriminator: An Adversarially Maintained Lightweight Benchmark for LLM Evaluation
proposals.motivation: Large-scale human evaluation platforms like LMSys Chatbot Arena provide reliable rankings but are prohibitively expensive and slow, hindering rapid model iteration. Existing static benchmarks are quickly saturated and gamed, losing correlation with human preference. There is a critical need for a cost-effective, dynamic evaluation suite that maintains high fidelity to comprehensive human judgments as models evolve.
proposals.hypothesis: A lightweight benchmark, dynamically curated through adversarial mining of hard, discriminating examples from a live arena and vetted by a small panel of trusted models, can maintain a high Spearman rank correlation (>0.95) with the full Arena's rankings while reducing required human votes by over 99%.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage system. First, a continuous data mining pipeline analyzes Chatbot Arena logs to identify 'adversarial' prompts where top-tier models (e.g., GPT-4, Claude) produce divergent, high-quality responses, using disagreement and entropy metrics. Second, a novel vetting mechanism employs a fixed, trusted 'guardian' panel (e.g., a curated set of older, strong models) to filter mined prompts, ensuring they remain discriminating against new model submissions. The benchmark is refreshed weekly via an online optimization that maximizes discriminative power per prompt.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver an open-source, automated system for maintaining a robust, efficient proxy for human evaluation, drastically lowering the cost of reliable model assessment. It will provide new insights into the dynamics of benchmark obsolescence and a method for creating 'evergreen' evaluation sets resistant to overfitting.
proposals.novelty: Unlike static datasets or simple distillation, our method frames benchmark maintenance as an adversarial, online optimization problem directly tied to a live human feedback loop. It leverages the researcher's expertise in efficient serving systems to scalably mine and manage this dynamic data pipeline.

proposals.title: Arena-S: A Secure, Self-Hosted LLM Evaluation Platform with Adaptive Offloading for Variable Enterprise Workloads
proposals.motivation: Enterprises require confidential, accurate evaluation of internal LLMs against realistic, variable workloads (e.g., irregular user traffic, data pipelines) to guide deployment. Existing solutions force a trade-off: public benchmarks like Chatbot Arena risk data exposure and use static workloads, while on-premises systems with static offloading (e.g., vLLM, HuggingFace TGI) suffer severe performance degradation under dynamic batch sizes, failing to capture true production behavior. A secure, efficient platform for private, workload-faithful evaluation is missing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an adaptive offloading scheduler, which dynamically optimizes memory and compute transfer strategies between GPU, CPU, and SSD in real-time based on batch size fluctuations, can enable high-throughput, low-latency evaluation of sensitive enterprise workloads on private infrastructure, matching the efficiency of cloud benchmarks without the data risk.
proposals.method: We will build Arena-S, extending the Prism architecture for multi-model serving to a confidential evaluation setting. The core is a novel Adaptive Hierarchical Offloading Manager (AHOM) that monitors batch size distributions and model activation patterns. AHOM employs a lightweight reinforcement learning agent to dynamically select between GPU memory caching, CPU-pinned buffer strategies, and SSD paging, optimizing for a weighted objective of throughput and tail latency. The scheduler will be integrated with a locality-aware fair queue (inspired by prior fairness work) to manage concurrent evaluation jobs across multiple internal models under the variable load.
proposals.contribution: 1) A turnkey, open-source platform for secure, production-faithful LLM evaluation within enterprise firewalls. 2) A novel adaptive offloading scheduler that dynamically reconfigures memory hierarchies for variable batches, quantified by a new metric of 'evaluation throughput fidelity'. 3) Empirical analysis of trade-offs between privacy, evaluation accuracy, and efficiency for sensitive workloads.
proposals.novelty: This work uniquely fuses the confidential evaluation paradigm of Chatbot Arena with the dynamic resource sharing of Prism and the scheduling policies of fairness research, creating the first system designed specifically for variable-batch, private benchmarking—a distinct gap between static cloud benchmarks and generic variable-batch serving systems.

proposals.title: Activation-Aware Scheduling and Swapping for Memory-Efficient Multi-Model LLM Serving
proposals.motivation: Current LLM serving systems, like vLLM and Prism, focus heavily on optimizing KV cache memory, which dominates for long-context, high-throughput scenarios. However, for serving mixtures of smaller, specialized models or for tasks with short sequences but large batch sizes, activation memory and model parameters can become the primary bottlenecks. This shift in the dominant memory consumer necessitates a new class of scheduling and memory management strategies.
