proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a serving system which dynamically profiles and classifies jobs based on their dominant memory type (KV cache vs. activation/parameters) can employ a hybrid scheduler and a tiered memory manager to significantly improve GPU utilization and throughput in multi-model serving clusters.
proposals.method: We will design a system that: 1) Profiles jobs to build a cost model predicting whether KV cache or activation/parameter memory is dominant. 2) Implements a hybrid scheduler using fair, locality-aware scheduling (inspired by Locality-aware Fair Scheduling) for KV-dominant jobs, and a packing-based scheduler for activation-dominant jobs to maximize SM utilization. 3) Develops a tiered memory manager that selectively swaps out less-critical tensors (e.g., activations of idle models or less-frequently accessed model layers) to CPU or NVMe, prioritizing KV cache retention for latency-sensitive, long-context requests.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic analysis and solution for serving scenarios where non-KV-cache memory is the bottleneck. We will deliver a new scheduling policy and memory manager that increases cluster-wide throughput and GPU utilization for diverse, multi-model workloads, extending the efficiency frontier of LLM serving systems.
proposals.novelty: While existing systems optimize for a single dominant memory type (KV cache), our work is novel in dynamically identifying the bottleneck and applying distinct, optimized strategies for KV-cache-heavy and activation/parameter-heavy jobs within a unified serving framework.

proposals.title: MEMSYS: A Co-optimized Memory Management System for Multi-LLM Serving
proposals.motivation: In multi-LLM serving clusters, GPU memory is the primary bottleneck for throughput and cost, as models must be resident for low-latency inference. Existing systems like vLLM or Prism manage memory or compute in isolation, leading to suboptimal utilization when other resources like I/O bandwidth or inter-GPU communication become contended. A holistic scheduler is needed to treat memory not as a static allocation but as a dynamic resource to be traded off against these other constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a scheduler which explicitly co-optimizes GPU memory management with compute, I/O, and inter-GPU bandwidth constraints can achieve significantly higher aggregate throughput and fairness in multi-LLM serving clusters compared to systems that treat these resources independently.
proposals.method: We will design MEMSYS, a centralized scheduler that extends a locality-aware fair scheduler with a multi-resource cost model. It will use a predictive model of memory pressure, I/O latency (for paging/swapping), and inter-GPU transfer times to make joint placement and batching decisions. Key algorithms include a memory-aware version of the Continuous Batching algorithm that can dynamically swap out less-critical model layers or KV cache segments to CPU memory when inter-GPU bandwidth is saturated, and a gradient-based scheduling policy that balances fairness across multiple resource dimensions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a unified model for multi-resource (memory, compute, I/O, bandwidth) scheduling in LLM serving, 2) the MEMSYS prototype demonstrating superior cluster-wide throughput and fairness, and 3) a set of scheduling primitives for explicit memory-compute-bandwidth trade-offs, enabling more cost-efficient serving of diverse model portfolios.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., Prism) shares GPUs and some systems manage paging, MEMSYS is novel in its integrated optimization across all major data center resources, treating GPU memory as a fluid, transferable asset to actively relieve other bottlenecks, not just a capacity limit.

proposals.title: PagedKV: Virtualizing KV Cache for Efficient Multi-Model Speculative Decoding
proposals.motivation: Speculative decoding and parallel token generation schemes promise to break the sequential bottleneck of LLM inference, but they dramatically increase the memory footprint of the Key-Value (KV) cache. This is a critical barrier for cost-effective multi-LLM serving, as seen in systems like Prism, where GPU memory is a scarce, shared resource. Existing KV cache management is monolithic and inefficient for these emerging, irregular generation patterns.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying virtual memory paging principles to the KV cache—decoupling its logical allocation from its physical GPU residency—can enable efficient, high-throughput multi-LLM serving with speculative decoding, while maintaining strict latency Service Level Objectives (SLOs).
proposals.method: We will design PagedKV, a virtualized KV cache manager integrated into a serving system. It will treat the KV cache of each request as a logical space, with a lightweight scheduler (inspired by locality-aware fairness) determining which 'pages' of the cache reside in fast GPU memory versus slower CPU DRAM. We will develop a novel eviction and prefetching policy that anticipates the access patterns of speculative decoding trees and parallel draft models, minimizing the performance penalty of swapping.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new systems abstraction for managing the KV cache, directly increasing the number of concurrent LLM requests or speculative batches a GPU can support. It will provide the first quantitative analysis of the memory-latency trade-offs in speculative serving systems and release an open-source integration with a major serving framework.
proposals.novelty: While OS paging is a classic technique, its application to the unique, structured, and performance-critical domain of the LLM KV cache for speculative execution is novel. It moves beyond static partitioning or simple eviction to a holistic virtual memory system tailored for irregular, multi-model inference graphs.

proposals.title: UniCache: Transparent, Locality-Aware KV Cache Management for Disaggregated LLM Serving
proposals.motivation: The exploding memory demands of LLM inference, driven by long-context KV caches, are bottlenecked by scarce and expensive GPU HBM. Emerging disaggregated systems with heterogeneous memory tiers (HBM, host DDR, CXL-attached memory) offer a cost-effective solution, but existing LLM serving systems lack efficient, transparent mechanisms to manage the KV cache across these tiers with vastly different latencies and bandwidths. This creates a critical gap between hardware capability and software efficiency for multi-LLM, high-throughput serving.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a unified, locality-aware KV cache manager can transparently leverage heterogeneous memory in disaggregated systems, dynamically placing and migrating cache blocks based on access patterns to achieve near-HBM performance at a significantly lower cost per GPU.
proposals.method: We will design UniCache, a system integrated into a serving runtime like vLLM or Orca. UniCache implements a unified virtual address space for KV cache across HBM, DDR, and CXL memory. It uses a lightweight, frequency-based tracking mechanism to classify cache blocks as hot, warm, or cold. A dynamic migration daemon, informed by this classification and hardware performance counters, asynchronously moves blocks between tiers. We will develop a novel scheduling policy, extending prior fairness work, that co-optimizes request-level SLOs with the data movement costs of fetching KV blocks from slower memory tiers.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver 1) a production-grade, open-source KV cache manager for disaggregated memory, 2) a formal model and scheduling policy for latency-cost trade-offs in heterogeneous memory systems, and 3) a comprehensive evaluation demonstrating cost savings and throughput improvements for multi-LLM workloads on simulated and real hardware prototypes.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior GPU sharing or memory scheduling work, UniCache explicitly targets the unique access patterns and SLO requirements of LLM KV caches in a disaggregated setting, providing full transparency to the inference engine while co-designing the scheduler and cache manager.

proposals.title: PagedAttention++: Co-designing KV Cache Management with Model Parallelism for Efficient Giant LLM Serving
proposals.motivation: The demand for serving massive models (e.g., >1T parameters) necessitates model parallelism (MP), but existing MP strategies (tensor, pipeline, expert) are designed for training, creating inefficiencies during inference. The key bottleneck is the fragmented management of the attention key-value (KV) cache across devices, which clashes with the memory-sharing and preemption capabilities of high-throughput serving systems like vLLM. This mismatch severely limits throughput and increases serving cost for state-of-the-art models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by co-designing vLLM's PagedAttention memory manager with model parallelism strategies, we can create a unified scheduling and memory abstraction that dramatically improves GPU utilization and reduces latency for multi-tenant, giant-model inference.
proposals.method: We will extend the vLLM serving engine to be parallelism-aware. First, we will design a distributed PagedAttention protocol that allows non-contiguous KV cache 'pages' to be logically contiguous across MP devices, enabling efficient block-level sharing and swapping. Second, we will develop a locality-aware, global scheduler that coordinates request placement and pipeline execution across MP sub-clusters, integrating fairness constraints from prior work. Third, we will implement and evaluate this system, called PagedAttention++, supporting hybrid parallelism (e.g., pipeline-within-tensor) for models like Mixture-of-Experts, measuring end-to-end throughput and latency on real-world traces.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first high-throughput serving system natively integrated with multiple model parallelism strategies, enabling cost-effective serving of trillion-parameter models. It will provide a formal model for joint scheduling of compute and memory in a parallelized inference setting and release open-source modifications to the vLLM codebase.
proposals.novelty: While vLLM manages memory within a single GPU, and MP frameworks manage computation across GPUs, no system before 2026 cohesively unifies the two for dynamic, multi-request inference. The novelty lies in treating the distributed KV cache as a single, virtualized paged memory space managed jointly with parallel execution schedules.

proposals.title: Mosaic: A Multi-Objective Scheduler for Heterogeneous LLM Serving on vLLM
proposals.motivation: The rise of multi-tenant LLM serving platforms, where diverse models (e.g., large vs. small, general vs. specialized) are co-located on shared GPU clusters, creates a complex optimization challenge. Existing schedulers like those in vLLM focus on single-model throughput or simple fairness, but fail to jointly optimize for system-wide metrics like aggregate throughput, memory efficiency, and tenant-level SLOs when models have vastly different computational and memory footprints.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a scheduler integrating locality-aware memory sharing with multi-objective optimization can significantly improve aggregate cluster throughput and memory utilization for heterogeneous model workloads, without violating per-tenant performance guarantees.
proposals.method: We will design and implement Mosaic, a new scheduling layer for vLLM. It will profile models for their KV cache patterns and memory demands. Using a two-tier policy, it will first perform locality-aware batch formation to group requests for the same or architecturally similar models to maximize PagedAttention efficiency and enable cross-request KV cache sharing. Second, it will employ a multi-armed bandit-based dispatcher that dynamically allocates GPU resources among model pools, optimizing for a weighted objective of global throughput, memory savings (inspired by Prism), and a fairness measure derived from per-tenant request latency percentiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, implementable scheduling framework for multi-model serving in vLLM, 2) a public extension to the vLLM codebase, and 3) a comprehensive evaluation using trace-driven simulations and a real testbed with diverse open-source models (e.g., Llama, Mistral, CodeLlama) showing improvements in key metrics over baseline vLLM and round-robin schedulers.
proposals.novelty: While prior work like Prism explores sharing and fairness in isolation, Mosaic uniquely combines model-aware locality scheduling with online multi-objective resource allocation specifically for the vLLM serving architecture, targeting the heterogeneity problem in production multi-tenant settings.

proposals.title: Fragmentation Bounds: A Formal Framework for Memory Efficiency in Multi-LLM Serving Systems
proposals.motivation: Multi-LLM serving systems like Prism and vLLM use dynamic GPU memory sharing to improve throughput and cost-efficiency. However, the fragmentation of memory from concurrent, variable-length requests leads to unpredictable waste and performance degradation. A formal, theoretical understanding of this trade-off is missing, hindering the design of systems with robust performance guarantees.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a given request arrival pattern and a set of LLM memory footprints, it is possible to derive tight theoretical bounds on worst-case memory fragmentation and its impact on key performance metrics like request latency and system throughput.
proposals.method: We will model the serving system as a dynamic bin-packing problem, where requests (items) of known sizes (model weights + KV cache) arrive and depart over time. We will analyze fragmentation under different scheduling policies (e.g., fair sharing, locality-aware). Using competitive analysis and adversarial queuing theory, we will derive upper and lower bounds on memory waste and request completion time for both online and batched scheduling algorithms, validating our models via discrete-event simulation of workloads derived from Chatbot Arena traces.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal framework to quantify the inherent trade-offs between memory sharing efficiency and scheduling fairness in LLM serving. It will yield actionable design principles and provable guarantees for system architects, moving the field from heuristic optimizations to principled system design.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., Prism) demonstrates empirical benefits of sharing, and scheduling papers address fairness, no pre-2026 research provides a rigorous theoretical analysis of fragmentation bounds for the specific, structured memory allocation patterns of LLM serving.

proposals.title: CoherenceKV: A Cache Coherence Protocol for Shared KV Blocks in Multi-GPU LLM Serving
proposals.motivation: Multi-GPU LLM serving systems like Prism share KV cache blocks across concurrent requests to improve memory efficiency and throughput. However, managing these shared blocks across GPU memories currently relies on ad-hoc, coarse-grained synchronization, leading to high overheads and underutilization. A principled, low-latency mechanism for maintaining consistency of shared KV states is a critical missing piece for scalable, high-throughput multi-LLM serving.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting classical multi-processor cache coherence protocols (e.g., directory-based MESI) to manage shared KV blocks across GPU memories will significantly reduce synchronization overheads, improve GPU memory utilization, and thereby increase aggregate serving throughput for multi-LLM workloads.
proposals.method: We will design CoherenceKV, a software-managed directory-based coherence protocol for KV blocks distributed across GPU memories. A central directory on the host will track block state (Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid) and ownership. We will implement lightweight RPC handlers on each GPU to service coherence requests (e.g., read, write, invalidate) and integrate these with the PagedAttention scheduler. The protocol will be evaluated by extending the vLLM or Orca serving system, measuring its impact on throughput and latency for mixed workloads on chatbot arena-style benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first formal cache coherence protocol for distributed KV caches in LLM serving, providing a systematic framework for shared memory management. It will demonstrate measurable gains in throughput and cost-efficiency for multi-model serving clusters, advancing the state-of-the-art in inference systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work shares KV blocks, it lacks a formal coherence model, treating consistency as an afterthought. Applying well-studied hardware coherence principles to this new software-defined domain is a novel cross-layer systems insight.

proposals.title: MINT: Memory-Intelligent Scheduling for Throughput-Constrained Multi-LLM Serving
proposals.motivation: Current LLM serving systems, including Ying Sheng's prior work on Prism, primarily optimize for maximizing throughput given fixed GPU memory. However, in cost-sensitive multi-tenant clouds and on-premises clusters, operators often have a target aggregate throughput and need to minimize the expensive GPU memory footprint to meet it. Pre-2026 systems lack first-class support for this inverse, memory-minimization objective.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by co-designing a memory-aware batch scheduler with a dynamic, locality-sensitive model placement policy, we can guarantee a specified throughput target while significantly reducing the aggregate GPU memory requirement compared to state-of-the-art throughput-maximizing schedulers.
proposals.method: We will develop MINT, a two-layer scheduler. First, a global model placement controller will use a constrained optimization formulation, informed by historical request patterns (like those from Chatbot Arena), to pack multiple LLMs onto GPUs, maximizing inter-request locality and model sharing. Second, a per-GPU batch scheduler will extend locality-aware fair scheduling concepts to prioritize requests that maximize memory reuse (e.g., via PagedAttention) and hit in-GPU model caches, explicitly trading off slight latency increases to stay within a memory budget while meeting the throughput target. We will implement a prototype by modifying vLLM and evaluate it against Prism and Orca.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic framework for memory-minimizing LLM serving, delivering concrete cost savings for cloud providers. It will contribute a new scheduling paradigm that treats memory as the primary optimization variable, alongside novel algorithms for throughput-constrained model placement and batch construction.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the classic serving optimization problem—from throughput-maximization under memory constraints to memory-minimization under throughput constraints—and co-designing the scheduler and placer to achieve this goal, which is unexplored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: EdgePagedAttention: Memory-Aware KV Cache Management for LLM Inference on Resource-Constrained Devices
proposals.motivation: The deployment of large language models (LLMs) on edge devices like smartphones and microcontrollers is critical for privacy, latency, and accessibility, but is severely limited by their constrained and non-uniform memory hierarchies. Pre-2026 serving systems like vLLM's PagedAttention optimize for GPU VRAM, assuming large, contiguous memory spaces, which is invalid on edge hardware with limited RAM and flash storage. Efficiently serving LLMs at the edge requires a fundamental rethinking of KV cache management to handle severe memory fragmentation and capacity limits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by extending the PagedAttention paradigm with a hierarchical, locality-aware page management strategy that explicitly models device memory (RAM) and slower storage (flash), we can enable feasible LLM inference on memory-constrained edge devices with minimal performance degradation compared to in-memory baselines.
proposals.method: We will design EdgePagedAttention, a new KV cache manager that treats device RAM as a cache for pages stored in flash. We will develop a lightweight, predictive page eviction policy based on attention score heatmaps from recent queries (inspired by Locality-aware Fair Scheduling) to minimize flash accesses. The system will integrate with a modified lightweight runtime (e.g., a stripped-down MLC-LLM) to manage page faults and pre-fetching, and we will implement a sharing mechanism for immutable prompt caches across concurrent sessions (extending Prism's concepts) to improve aggregate throughput on multi-core edge SoCs.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel KV cache management algorithm tailored for heterogeneous edge memory, 2) an open-source prototype integrated with an edge-focused LLM runtime, and 3) an empirical evaluation on real edge devices (e.g., Raspberry Pi, smartphones) quantifying the trade-offs between model size, batch size, memory footprint, and latency, providing a blueprint for efficient edge LLM serving.
proposals.novelty: While PagedAttention manages GPU memory, and OS paging handles DRAM-flash, no pre-2026 work co-designs LLM attention semantics with the specific memory hierarchy and access patterns of edge devices to make LLM inference feasible under extreme (<4GB) memory constraints.

proposals.title: FlashFetch: Paging and Allocation Policies for Minimal First-Token Latency in LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: Current LLM serving systems, including vLLM and Prism, optimize for high-throughput batch processing, which introduces significant queuing and scheduling delays for individual, latency-critical requests. As LLMs are increasingly deployed in interactive applications like chatbots and copilots, minimizing Time to First Token (TTFT) for single-user requests is paramount for user experience, yet remains an underexplored systems challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by designing GPU memory paging and allocation policies explicitly for single-request latency, rather than batch throughput, we can significantly reduce TTFT by minimizing the time spent on KV cache population and model parameter loading.
proposals.method: We will design a new memory manager that integrates (1) a predictive, request-aware page prefetcher to load required model blocks into GPU memory before computation begins, based on a lightweight analysis of the prompt, and (2) a latency-optimal allocation algorithm that prioritizes contiguous, high-bandwidth memory regions for the initial prompt processing phase. We will implement these techniques as a modification to the vLLM scheduler and evaluate against baselines like vLLM and Prism using trace-driven simulations and real-world deployment on the Chatbot Arena platform.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic study and solution for single-request latency optimization in LLM serving. We will deliver a set of concrete paging and allocation policies, an open-source prototype, and a quantitative analysis of the TTFT vs. throughput trade-off, enabling more responsive interactive AI applications.
proposals.novelty: While prior work on fairness (e.g., locality-aware scheduling) and sharing (e.g., Prism) improves multi-tenant efficiency, our work uniquely shifts the core optimization target from aggregate throughput to single-request latency, requiring a fundamental rethinking of memory management primitives.

proposals.title: Beyond GPT-4: A Systematic Study of LLM-as-a-Judge Generalizability Across Model Architectures and Training Paradigms
proposals.motivation: LLM-as-a-judge has emerged as a scalable, cost-effective method for evaluating model outputs, central to platforms like Chatbot Arena. However, prior studies (pre-2026) predominantly validate this method using judges and candidates from similar model families (e.g., GPT variants). The generalizability of this evaluation paradigm across diverse architectures (e.g., encoder-decoder, mixture-of-experts, non-transformer) and training paradigms (e.g., instruction-tuned vs. base, open-source vs. proprietary) remains an open and critical question for the reliability of automated evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the reliability and bias of LLM-as-a-judge are significantly influenced by the architectural and training lineage of the judge model relative to the candidate models, leading to systematic over- or under-estimation of performance for certain model families.
proposals.method: We will design a controlled, large-scale benchmarking study. First, we will curate a diverse testbed of candidate LLMs spanning architectures (decoder-only, encoder-decoder, hybrid) and training data regimes. Second, we will select a panel of judge LLMs with similar diversity. Using a framework inspired by Prism's multi-LLM serving efficiency, we will orchestrate pairwise comparisons across thousands of prompts from established benchmarks. We will analyze judge agreement with human preferences (from Chatbot Arena-like data) and introduce novel metrics to quantify 'architectural bias' and 'paradigm affinity' in judge scores, controlling for confounding factors like judge capability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic map of LLM-as-a-judge reliability across the modern LLM landscape, identifying robust judge choices and dangerous blind spots. It will yield concrete guidelines for selecting judges to ensure fair and accurate model comparisons, directly informing evaluation practices for the research community and platforms reliant on automated judging.
proposals.novelty: While prior work assumes judge capability is the primary differentiator, we uniquely investigate how architectural and training paradigm mismatches introduce systematic bias, a dimension largely unexplored in pre-2026 LLM evaluation literature.

proposals.title: H2O-G: Hardware-Aware Hierarchical Cache Management for Efficient Multi-LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: The H2O (Heavy-Hitter Oracle) caching algorithm has proven effective for managing KV caches in multi-LLM serving, improving aggregate throughput. However, its eviction policy is agnostic to the distinct performance characteristics of modern GPU memory hierarchies, such as the high bandwidth but limited capacity of HBM and the ultra-fast but tiny SRAM (e.g., on-chip cache). This leads to suboptimal data placement and eviction decisions, leaving potential throughput and latency gains on the table.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hardware-aware H2O variant, which explicitly models the access latency and bandwidth differentials between GPU SRAM and HBM, can make superior eviction decisions, significantly improving the effective cache hit rate and reducing inference latency for a shared GPU serving multiple LLMs.
proposals.method: We will design H2O-G, which extends H2O with a two-tier priority score. We will instrument the serving system to profile the access latency and bandwidth costs for KV cache blocks in both SRAM and HBM. The eviction algorithm will be modified to prioritize keeping 'heavy-hitter' tokens in SRAM based on a composite score of access frequency and the measured latency penalty of HBM access. We will implement a lightweight profiler to dynamically adjust these scores and integrate the system into a vLLM-like serving framework for evaluation on NVIDIA H100/A100 GPUs.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, hardware-aware caching policy for LLM inference that directly optimizes for the GPU memory hierarchy. We expect to demonstrate measurable improvements in tokens/sec/dollar and reduced P99 latency in multi-LLM serving scenarios compared to baseline H2O. The system will provide a blueprint for co-designing caching algorithms with emerging hardware memory traits.
proposals.novelty: While prior work like H2O and vLLM's PagedAttention manage KV cache efficiency, and Prism explores GPU sharing, none have explicitly tailored the cache eviction logic to the performance asymmetry between GPU SRAM and HBM. Our approach moves beyond capacity management to optimize for access latency and bandwidth, a novel direction in LLM serving systems.

proposals.title: CacheQuant: Co-designing Heavy-Hitter Caching and Quantized KV Representations for High-Throughput Multi-LLM Serving
proposals.motivation: The KV cache is a primary memory bottleneck in LLM serving, limiting throughput and increasing cost, especially in multi-model settings. Existing solutions like H2O's heavy-hitter caching reduce memory pressure but retain full-precision KV entries, while quantization techniques shrink KV size but apply uniformly, degrading accuracy for critical attention heads. A hybrid approach is needed to maximize memory efficiency without sacrificing quality for high-impact tokens.
proposals.hypothesis: By selectively applying aggressive quantization to non-heavy-hitter KV cache entries while preserving high precision for cached heavy hitters, we can achieve superior memory-throughput trade-offs compared to either technique alone, with minimal impact on generation quality.
proposals.method: We will extend the H2O framework with a precision-aware caching policy. The system will profile attention patterns to identify heavy-hitter tokens, storing their KV vectors in FP16 within a shared GPU cache. For all other tokens, we will apply per-token, per-head quantization (e.g., to INT4/INT8) using recent learned quantization schemes. A lightweight, online classifier will route incoming tokens to the high-precision cache or the quantized KV workspace. We will implement this within a modified vLLM or Orca scheduler to evaluate multi-LLM workloads.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel serving system that significantly increases the number of concurrent LLM requests or models per GPU by co-optimizing caching and quantization. It will provide a systematic analysis of the interaction between token importance and quantization sensitivity, offering new principles for memory-efficient inference.
proposals.novelty: While H2O and quantization are established, their integration is not straightforward; this proposal introduces a non-uniform quantization strategy dictated by cacheability, moving beyond static, model-wide precision reduction. The co-design explicitly leverages the statistical skew in attention to guide precision allocation, a novel systems-for-ML optimization.

proposals.title: H2O at Scale: Evaluating a Memory-Efficient KV Cache Policy for Million-Token LLM Contexts
proposals.motivation: As LLMs are applied to long-document analysis, code repositories, and extended dialogues, supporting context windows exceeding 1 million tokens is a critical frontier. However, the GPU memory required for the Key-Value (KV) cache grows linearly with context length, making standard attention mechanisms prohibitively expensive. Efficient KV cache eviction policies are essential to make massive-context inference feasible on existing hardware.
proposals.hypothesis: The H2O (Heavy-Hitter Oracle) KV cache eviction policy, which retains tokens with the highest attention scores, will maintain acceptable model quality (e.g., perplexity, task accuracy) on sequences of over 1 million tokens when only a small, fixed-size cache can be stored, thereby enabling massive-context serving.
proposals.method: We will implement and test the H2O policy within a high-throughput LLM serving system (extending frameworks like vLLM or building on Prism's sharing concepts). We will evaluate on models like Llama 2 or CodeLlama, scaled to 1M+ token contexts using positional encoding extrapolation techniques (e.g., YaRN, PI). We will design benchmarks using long-context datasets (e.g., PG-19, LongBench) and synthetic tasks, measuring throughput, latency, and quality metrics against a full-cache baseline and other eviction policies (e.g., FIFO, random).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous evaluation of a learned KV cache policy at the million-token scale, establishing empirical bounds on the trade-off between cache size and model performance. It will deliver open-source system modifications and benchmarks for massive-context inference, directly informing the design of future cost-efficient LLM serving systems.
proposals.novelty: While H2O has been proposed for moderate contexts, its behavior and efficacy at the extreme scale of >1M tokens is unknown and non-trivial, as attention patterns may shift fundamentally. This work bridges the gap between algorithmic cache policy research and the practical systems challenge of massive-context serving.

proposals.title: Downscale: A Hierarchical Eviction Framework for Memory-Constrained On-Device Tiny LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: The deployment of tiny LLMs on mobile devices is critical for privacy, latency, and accessibility, but is severely hampered by limited and non-uniform memory hierarchies (e.g., RAM vs. flash). Existing GPU-centric eviction policies like H2O are designed for server-scale VRAM and fail to account for the unique access patterns and costs of mobile hardware, leading to excessive swapping and poor responsiveness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a novel, hierarchical eviction policy, co-designed with the memory access patterns of quantized tiny LLMs and the cost model of mobile memory subsystems, can significantly improve inference throughput and reduce latency under stringent, sub-4GB memory budgets.
proposals.method: We will design Downscale, a two-tier eviction manager for on-device inference. First, we will profile the temporal locality of attention keys/values and feed-forward network activations for models like Phi-2 or TinyLlama. Second, we will develop a cost-aware scheduler that prioritizes evicting blocks with the lowest expected future access cost, weighted by the penalty of retrieval from slower storage (e.g., flash). This extends beyond LRU by incorporating a learned model of layer-specific and sequence-position-specific access patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a new open-source eviction framework for mobile LLM runtimes, 2) a characterization of tiny LLM memory access patterns under constraint, and 3) empirical results demonstrating substantially higher effective batch size and lower P99 latency compared to baseline policies on real mobile hardware.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., Prism) optimizes GPU sharing for large models, Downscale is the first to tailor eviction specifically for the severe, heterogeneous memory constraints of mobile devices running tiny LLMs, moving beyond server-inspired caching.

ideas.title: Integration with model parallelism strategies
ideas.description: Combine vLLM's memory management with tensor, pipeline, or expert parallelism for massive models.
ideas.sourceTitle: Efficient Memory Management for Large Language Model Serving with PagedAttention

ideas.title: Adaptive Offloading for Latency-Sensitive Model Discrimination
ideas.description: This research develops a closed-loop system that integrates a high-throughput offloading engine (like FlexGen) with an adaptive question generator. The system targets interactive applications by using the generator to create low-latency, maximally discriminative test prompts that probe the specific performance boundaries (e.g., memory bandwidth, compute latency) of the LLM when offloaded. The results from executing these probes are then fed back to dynamically optimize the offloading scheduler's parameters—such as chunk size, prefetch depth, and layer placement—in real-time, tailoring the engine to the unique characteristics of both the model and the hardware for optimal interactive performance.

ideas.title: GPU memory is the only scarce resource

ideas.title: Extend FlexGen to latency-sensitive tasks

ideas.title: Architecture-Aware Framework for Deriving Optimal Tensor Placement and Compression Strategies
ideas.description: This research develops a principled framework to systematically adapt tensor optimization methods to diverse model architectures, moving beyond naive transfer. It first introduces a formal graph-based abstraction to model the dataflow, operator dependencies, and memory access patterns unique to architectures like SSMs, MoEs, and RNNs. Using this model, the system then employs a hybrid analytical and search-based optimizer to automatically generate and validate architecture-specific placement and compression policies. The outcome is a robust methodology that guarantees efficient resource utilization across model families by deriving optimizations from first principles tailored to each architectural paradigm.

ideas.title: Adapting Just-In-Time Compilation for Latency-Sensitive Model Offloading
ideas.description: This idea proposes to adapt the high-throughput offloading engine of FlexGen for low-latency, interactive applications by drawing an analogy from compiler optimization. In compiler design, Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation solves the analogous trade-off between optimization time and execution latency by initially using a faster, simpler execution mode while profiling to identify and subsequently cache optimized versions of hot code paths. Adapted to this context, the research would develop a JIT-inspired offloading scheduler. The system would begin inference with a conservative, latency-first execution strategy to ensure responsiveness. In parallel, it would profile the computational graph and request characteristics. For frequent, predictable inference patterns, it would generate and memoize highly optimized, throughput-oriented offloading plans, dynamically shifting the system's performance profile from low-latency to high-throughput based on observed workload stability.

ideas.title: Adaptive Offloading for Private Enterprise Evaluation of Variable-Workload Models
ideas.description: This research develops a self-hosted, confidential evaluation platform for enterprises that integrates a novel offloading framework designed for dynamic batch sizes. The core innovation is an adaptive memory and compute scheduler that dynamically adjusts GPU-CPU/SSD data transfer strategies in real-time based on the fluctuating batch sizes of live, sensitive enterprise data (e.g., per-user inference requests, irregular internal data pipelines). This enables companies to perform accurate, private benchmarking of their internal models against realistic, variable workloads without the performance penalties of static offloading or the data exposure risks of public cloud platforms. The result is a turnkey, on-premises evaluation arena that mirrors production traffic patterns with high efficiency, a capability neither uniform-batch cloud benchmarks nor generic variable-batch techniques provide in a secure, enterprise context.

ideas.title: Optimize H2O for hardware-specific memory hierarchies
ideas.description: Tailor the cache eviction algorithm for GPU HBM and SRAM characteristics.
ideas.sourceTitle: H2O: Heavy-Hitter Oracle for Efficient Generative Inference of Large Language Models

ideas.title: Efficiency-Aware Continuous A/B Testing for Adaptive Model Selection
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a system that continuously runs large-scale A/B tests, not just to compare models on accuracy or throughput, but to rigorously measure their total computational efficiency (e.g., energy per inference, FLOPs per task) across diverse real-world workloads and user segments. By framing efficiency as a primary, segment-specific optimization target, the system can automatically route queries or deploy the model variant that achieves the best Pareto-optimal balance of task performance and resource consumption for a given context. This moves beyond static model comparisons to enable adaptive, cost-aware inference pipelines that dynamically optimize for overall system efficiency without sacrificing tailored quality of service.

ideas.title: Predictive Prefetching and Tiered Orchestration for Latency-Hidden Disk-Based Inference
ideas.description: This research designs a system that makes disk a viable memory tier for interactive inference by completely hiding its access latency. It co-designs a lightweight runtime scheduler with the model architecture to predict future parameter and KV cache accesses, triggering aggressive prefetching from disk into a faster buffer tier. The system employs a tiered memory manager (DRAM, NVMe, Disk) that orchestrates data movement based on real-time access predictions, not just recent usage. The goal is to achieve interactive latencies by ensuring the compute engine never waits for disk, transforming the disk's role from a direct data source to a background, high-capacity reservoir for a predictive loading system.

ideas.title: Direct Execution of Compressed Weights for Seamless Offloading
ideas.description: This system integrates weight offloading with compression techniques designed for direct execution, eliminating decompression overhead. It uses a block-wise sparse-quantized format where weights are stored and swapped in a fixed, hardware-friendly layout that GPU kernels can compute with directly. The offloading scheduler is aware of this format, fetching compressed blocks and mapping them to specialized kernels that perform sparse, low-precision arithmetic without a separate decompression step. This ensures the memory savings from compression and offloading translate directly into end-to-end latency and throughput improvements.

ideas.title: Adaptive Buffer Pooling for Dynamic Batch Offloading
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of uniform batch sizes in offloading frameworks by drawing an analogy from TCP/IP flow control in computer networking. In networking, TCP handles variable packet sizes and arrival rates using dynamic receive window sizing and buffering, where the receiver advertises available buffer space to regulate sender transmission. Adapting this solution, we propose implementing an adaptive buffer pooling mechanism for GPU offloading that dynamically allocates memory slots for variable-sized batches. Instead of requiring uniform batches, the system would use a credit-based flow control approach where the CPU (host) signals available buffer capacity to the GPU (device), enabling efficient processing of irregular batch chunks. This would allow the offloading framework to handle dynamic batch sizes without stalling, memory waste, or the need for padding, similar to how TCP handles bursty network traffic while preventing buffer overflow.

ideas.title: Minimize memory for fixed throughput target

ideas.title: Active CPU-Guided Generation of Discriminative Model Probes
ideas.description: This research investigates a novel benchmarking framework where the CPU is not a passive memory extension but an active computational agent that guides an LLM to generate optimal, discriminative test questions. The CPU continuously analyzes the comparative performance of target LLMs on initial probes, using this feedback to dynamically formulate new, more challenging questions that stress specific capabilities like long-context reasoning or factual recall. This creates a closed-loop system where question generation adapts in real-time to probe the evolving performance landscape, yielding deeper insights into model architectures than static benchmarks. The core synergy is using active CPU computation to turn question generation into an adaptive, diagnostic tool for uncovering the fundamental computational roles of different model components.

ideas.title: CPU as a Computational Co-processor During Generation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that the CPU's role during generation by a dedicated accelerator (e.g., GPU, TPU) is passive, limited to memory extension and data fetching. Drawing an analogy from cellular biology, where the endoplasmic reticulum performs active processing (folding, quality control) concurrently with its transport function, we adapt this to computer architecture. The research would investigate designing CPU cores or dedicated units on the CPU die to act as a 'computational co-processor' to the main accelerator. During the generation phase, as data moves from main memory or cache towards the accelerator, the CPU would perform active, lightweight computations on the data stream—such as dynamic pruning, data structure reorganization, or preconditioning—effectively overlapping memory access with useful computation and reducing the post-fetch processing burden on the accelerator.

ideas.title: Optimization for multi-tenant, multi-model serving
ideas.description: Develop scheduling policies for shared clusters running diverse models with vLLM.
ideas.sourceTitle: Efficient Memory Management for Large Language Model Serving with PagedAttention

ideas.title: Dynamic Batch A/B Testing for Adaptive Model Offloading
ideas.description: This research fuses dynamic batch size handling with A/B testing frameworks to create an adaptive model offloading system. It treats incoming inference requests with variable batch sizes as natural, continuously running A/B tests, where different model variants or hardware configurations are evaluated on-the-fly. The system efficiently allocates variable-sized request batches to different model 'arms' based on segment characteristics (e.g., query complexity, user tier), using the inherent variability in load to gather robust performance metrics. This allows for continuous optimization of offloading strategies, automatically routing workloads to the most cost-effective or performant model-hardware pairing for each dynamically identified segment.

ideas.title: CPU-Accelerated Online Learning for Real-Time Model Ranking and Selection
ideas.description: This research investigates offloading the continuous online learning process for dynamic model Elo scores to the CPU, transforming it from a passive memory manager into an active, concurrent learning agent. As a GPU generates text from a selected model, the CPU uses the incoming stream of human or automated feedback (votes) to update competitive rankings and skill estimates in real-time using algorithms like online gradient descent or bandit strategies. This tight integration allows the system to make near-instantaneous routing decisions for the next generation task, dynamically selecting the currently best-performing model from a pool without pausing the main GPU workload, thereby optimizing overall system throughput and quality adaptively.

ideas.title: Tournament-Aware Tiering for Interactive Model Ranking
ideas.description: This research explores methods to rank a vast number of machine learning models—too large to fit in RAM—through interactive, pairwise comparisons for tasks like benchmark evaluation or preference tuning. It combines the challenge of using disks as a viable memory tier for live model loading with optimized tournament pairing strategies from sports statistics. The core synergy is designing tournament brackets that are cognizant of disk locality and access patterns, strategically pairing models stored contiguously or in the same fetch operation to minimize I/O latency. This results in a system that achieves statistically confident model rankings with far fewer total disk seeks, making large-scale interactive inference tournaments practically feasible.

ideas.title: Optimize for latency-critical single requests

ideas.title: Dynamic Model-Aware Offloading for Continuous Learning Systems
ideas.description: This research designs an efficient offloading system specifically for models that are continuously learning or being updated, rejecting the static model assumption. It introduces a runtime layer that tracks fine-grained model deltas (parameter changes, structural modifications, optimizer state updates) and integrates this metadata into the offloading scheduler. The system performs predictive and differential data movement, offloading only changed components and pre-fetching likely future states based on the learning trajectory. This approach ensures optimal memory and bandwidth usage that adapts in real-time to the model's evolution, making it robust for lifelong learning and frequent fine-tuning scenarios.

ideas.title: Hierarchical, Loss-Aware Model Compression via Analogous Signal Processing
ideas.description: This idea proposes a novel model compression approach for offloading systems like FlexGen, inspired by hierarchical compression in digital signal processing. Analogous to how MP3/JPEG transforms data and discards less perceptually significant high-frequency components, we transform model parameters into a basis where their importance to task accuracy is stratified. The adapted solution is to integrate this with offloading by applying sophisticated, lossy quantization (or even pruning) to the less important weight components that reside in slow, offloaded memory, while preserving high-fidelity representations for the critical components in faster memory. This creates a hybrid, multi-resolution weight representation that is co-designed with the memory hierarchy, moving beyond uniform quantization to a performance-aware, hierarchical compression scheme.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Real-Time Model Compression for Live A/B Testing of LLMs
ideas.description: This research integrates advanced weight compression techniques directly into a continuous deployment testing framework for LLM APIs. The system performs real-time A/B or canary testing of multiple model versions, where each version may use a different compression strategy (structured sparsity, mixed-precision quantization, FlexGen-style offloading). By comparing these variants on identical production queries, it collects live metrics on throughput, latency, accuracy, and hardware utilization to determine the optimal compression-configuration trade-off for the current workload. This enables automated, empirical optimization of compression techniques in real-world scenarios, moving beyond static offline benchmarks.

ideas.title: Efficient Tournament-Based Offloading for Dynamic Model Populations
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for intelligently offloading models from a central server to edge devices when the population of candidate models is dynamic, due to continuous learning or frequent updates. It applies optimized tournament design principles from sports statistics to structure pairwise comparisons between model versions, determining which are superior for a given task with minimal inference-based 'votes'. This allows the system to confidently identify a small, elite subset of high-performing models for offloading, while relegating inferior versions, thereby optimizing both the evaluation cost and the resource cost of model distribution.

ideas.title: Adaptive Caching for Dynamic Models using Library Versioning Principles
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that models are static and pre-trained by developing efficient offloading for continuously learning models, drawing an analogy from software library dependency management. In that field, systems handle frequent library updates using versioning, dependency graphs, and delta-based caching to avoid redundant transfers. The adapted solution treats a dynamically updated model like a versioned software library. It employs semantic versioning to track changes, caches model components (layers, parameter blocks) keyed by version hashes, and computes deltas between versions. When offloading an update, only the delta of changed parameters or sub-modules is transmitted. A resolver on the edge device reconstructs the updated model by combining cached components with the new delta, drastically reducing communication overhead and enabling efficient deployment of frequently updated models.

ideas.title: Dynamic Discriminator: An Adversarially Maintained Lightweight Benchmark
ideas.description: This system creates a lightweight evaluation suite that actively maintains high correlation with the full LMSys Arena by dynamically evolving its test questions. It employs a two-stage process: first, continuously mining the live Arena for hard, discriminating examples where top models disagree, and second, using a small panel of trusted 'guardian' models to vet and refresh the benchmark. This ensures the benchmark remains discriminating against new model generations and resistant to gaming, providing a robust, up-to-date proxy for the full Arena's ranking with orders of magnitude fewer required votes.

ideas.title: Architecture-Agnostic, Resource-Constrained Models for Real-Time Manipulation Detection
ideas.description: This research fuses the need for efficient, non-transformer model deployment with the challenge of detecting sophisticated vote manipulation. It proposes developing novel, lightweight model architectures (such as State Space Models or efficient MLP variants) that are intrinsically co-designed with adversarial detection heads. These models would be optimized via advanced tensor placement and compression techniques to run in real-time on resource-constrained hardware (e.g., edge servers, mobile devices). This enables continuous, on-device scrutiny of voting patterns and user behavior to identify manipulation, moving beyond centralized, computationally heavy transformer-based analysis and creating a new class of efficient, trustworthy guardrail models for distributed systems.

ideas.title: Architecture-Agnostic Tensor Optimization via Computational Fluid Dynamics Meshing Principles
ideas.description: This research seed proposes extending tensor placement and compression methods to other large model architectures beyond transformers. The analogy is drawn from Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), which solves the problem of simulating complex flows efficiently using adaptive mesh refinement (AMR)—dynamically adjusting grid resolution based on local flow properties to save resources while maintaining accuracy in critical areas. The adapted idea applies this principle by developing architecture-agnostic tensor optimization techniques. For models like State Space Models or Mixture of Experts, the solution involves dynamically analyzing the computational graph to identify 'low-activity' tensor regions suitable for aggressive compression or offloading, and 'high-variance' critical pathways where tensor fidelity must be preserved. This creates a non-uniform, adaptive optimization strategy that tailors resource allocation to the specific structural and dynamic properties of any given model family.

ideas.title: Task-Aware, Differentiable Co-Search for Accuracy-Constrained Neural Network Compression
ideas.description: This system performs a joint, automated search for mixed-precision schemes across weights and cache while directly optimizing for a user-specified accuracy or robustness constraint on the target task. It employs an inner loop that trains lightweight surrogate models to predict the accuracy impact of any candidate compression configuration, and an outer loop that searches the configuration space using these surrogates within a constrained optimization framework. This ensures the discovered compression policy is Pareto-optimal, balancing high compression ratios with guaranteed minimal degradation in the model's final application performance.

ideas.title: Effectiveness-Constrained Efficiency Optimization
ideas.description: This approach reframes the optimization target as minimizing total energy or computational resource consumption, but only for solutions that first satisfy a mandatory effectiveness criterion defined for the specific problem domain. The system dynamically trades off efficiency levers (like precision, algorithmic choice, or hardware configuration) against a hard constraint for solution quality or wall-clock time. This prevents the pursuit of efficiency from degenerating into useless computation, ensuring resources are saved only on valid, actionable outcomes. The core research challenge is developing co-optimization frameworks that can navigate this constrained design space across diverse workloads.

ideas.title: Learning a Latent Preference Space for Scalable, Data-Efficient Model Recommendation
ideas.description: This research moves beyond the assumption of a single global ranking by learning a structured, low-dimensional 'preference space' from a diverse but manageable set of benchmark evaluations. Models and user-task contexts are embedded into this space based on their performance profiles and requirements (e.g., cost sensitivity, accuracy on specific skills). The system can then predict a personalized model ranking for a novel user or task by locating it in this space and finding the nearest, best-performing models, eliminating the need for exhaustive evaluation of all models on all possible contexts.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Psychometrically-Informed Batch Construction for Efficient Pairwise Comparison Offloading
ideas.description: This research synthesizes Item Response Theory (IRT) for modeling question difficulty/discrimination with a framework supporting dynamic batch sizes. The core idea is to use real-time IRT parameter estimates to intelligently and adaptively construct variable-sized batches of pairwise comparison tasks for offloading (e.g., to crowdsourcing or distributed systems). Instead of uniform batches, the system forms batches where questions are grouped based on their current psychometric uncertainty and estimated computational cost, prioritizing comparisons that will most efficiently reduce parameter uncertainty. This enables a closed-loop system where batch composition and size are continuously optimized, leading to faster convergence in question calibration and more efficient use of variable or constrained computational resources per offloading round.

ideas.title: Personalized Lightweight Benchmarking for Task-Dependent Model Selection
ideas.description: This research fuses efficient benchmark design with personalized ranking to develop a system that constructs a minimal, diagnostic question set for a given user or task context. Instead of a single fixed benchmark, the system uses a small number of user preference probes or task examples to select a highly discriminating, personalized subset from a larger calibrated question bank. This allows for a lightweight, on-demand evaluation that approximates a full personalized Arena ranking with orders of magnitude fewer comparisons, making practical, tailored model recommendations feasible for individual developers and applications.

ideas.title: Live, Segmented A/B Feedback Loops for Adaptive Model Training
ideas.description: This research proposes a platform that integrates continuous, production-level A/B testing directly into the model training feedback loop. Instead of generic evaluation metrics, the system uses live A/B comparisons on distinct user segments or task types to generate granular, actionable performance signals. These signals are then fed back to the training process, allowing developers to iteratively steer model updates to improve performance on underperforming segments or to specialize models for different operational contexts. This creates a closed-loop system where model development is continuously guided by real-world, segmented effectiveness data, moving beyond static benchmarks to adaptive, context-aware optimization.

ideas.title: Safe Canary Gating via Shadow Production for Continuous LLM Deployment
ideas.description: This system integrates a mandatory safety buffer between model updates and live users by mirroring all production API traffic to a shadow environment. In this canary stage, new model versions process the mirrored traffic and are evaluated in real-time against the incumbent model using a comprehensive suite of metrics covering correctness, safety, latency, and cost. The model is only promoted to serve a fraction of real traffic if it passes all automated gates, preventing exposure to regressed or harmful outputs. This creates a continuous, zero-user-risk deployment pipeline that maintains the original goal of real-time testing on production data.

ideas.title: Evolutionary Search for Pareto-Optimal Mixed-Precision Compression
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from computational chemistry's drug discovery pipelines, specifically the use of multi-objective evolutionary algorithms (MOEAs), to the problem of automated search for optimal neural network compression ratios. In the analogous field, MOEAs efficiently navigate vast, combinatorial chemical spaces to find molecules that optimally trade off multiple competing objectives. The adapted idea is to frame the search for mixed-precision schemes (assigning precisions like FP16, INT8, INT4 to weights and cache) as a multi-objective optimization problem. An evolutionary algorithm will maintain a population of candidate precision schemes, apply mutation (e.g., changing the precision of a layer) and crossover (combining parts of different schemes) operators, and evaluate fitness based on a Pareto frontier defined by key metrics: model accuracy (fidelity), achieved compression ratio (model size), and inference latency/energy. This method automates the discovery of non-dominated, Pareto-optimal compression configurations tailored to specific hardware constraints and application requirements, moving beyond manual or brute-force search strategies.

ideas.title: Verifiable Private Evaluation via Confidential Computing Enclaves
ideas.description: This system enables enterprises to confidentially evaluate proprietary models on sensitive data using a self-hosted, zero-trust platform. The core innovation is the mandatory use of hardware-based trusted execution environments (e.g., Intel SGX, AMD SEV, or AWS Nitro Enclaves) for all evaluation workloads, ensuring data and models are never exposed in plaintext to the underlying infrastructure. A verifiable attestation protocol guarantees the integrity of the enclave and the evaluation code before any data is released for processing. This approach shifts the security boundary from the entire corporate network to the hardened silicon of the processor, dramatically reducing the attack surface and mitigating the risk of insider threats or infrastructure compromise.

ideas.title: Calibrated, Context-Aware Multi-Criteria Evaluation with Dynamic Criterion Selection
ideas.description: This redesigned system moves beyond simply adding criteria by ensuring they are applied reliably and efficiently. It first calibrates raters through quick, interactive tests for each criterion to align their judgments with predefined anchors. During evaluation, the system analyzes the AI response and the original prompt to dynamically select and present only the most pertinent 1-2 criteria for that specific interaction, such as 'safety' for a potentially harmful query or 'creativity' for an open-ended request. This approach minimizes rater fatigue, maximizes rating consistency, and produces higher-quality, more actionable fine-grained feedback for model training and benchmarking.
